title: Post rollout auto-rollback events to Slack (concise notification + links)
preview: Summary: incident-bot now posts a Slack notification when the rollout controller triggers an automatic safety action due to an SLO breach (auto_rollback, auto_pause, or rollback_suppressed). The message is intentionally short, includes the rollout_id and scope, and provides deep links to (1) the rollout in Console, (2) the rollout SLO dashboard, and (3) the audit event details. Motivation: during the 2026-02 simulations and recent customer escalations, oncall often learned about auto-mitigations only after secondary alerts or customer reports; this closes the loop and reduces time-to-context while keeping noise bounded. Event source: consumes the new structured audit event emitted to the event stream (redwood PR-28435) with event_type=rollout.auto_action.*. The handler maps event subtypes to Slack severity and routing without paging by default (notify-only) to avoid paging storms. Message format (example): "[AUTO-ROLLBACK] rollout=rol_01HT… scope=route:/v1/chat model=llama3.1-70b-v3 region=us-east tier=dedicated reason=p99_latency_burnrate_fast" followed by links: Console rollout, Grafana dashboard, Audit event (internal). Guardrails: (a) dedupe by (rollout_id, action, step_id) for 10 minutes, (b) drop messages if required links can’t be constructed (still increments a metric), (c) redacts customer identifiers (uses customer_tier + optional org_id hash only), (d) rate limits per channel to 1 msg/sec with burst=5. Routing: auto_rollback -> #eng-oncall (notify) + #eng-sre (notify); auto_pause -> #eng-platform (notify); rollback_suppressed -> thread reply on the most recent auto action message if present, otherwise #eng-sre (notify). Configuration: adds rollout_auto_actions.enabled (default false in prod; true in staging), rollout_auto_actions.channels overrides, and a link template block for Console/Grafana base URLs per environment. Observability: emits incident_bot_slack_posts_total{type=rollout_auto_action,outcome=sent|dropped|rate_limited|deduped} and incident_bot_rollout_auto_action_events_total{action=rollback|pause|suppressed}. Testing: unit tests cover payload parsing, formatting, dedupe, and redaction; an integration test uses a fixture event to validate generated Slack blocks; updated run instructions in README. Rollout plan: staging enablement first (paired with redwood PR-28490), then prod behind flag once dashboards/alerts are confirmed (observability-pack PR-913).

title: Add billing meter regression scenarios for streaming, retries, and fallback routing
preview:
Motivation: We’ve had multiple meter-related regressions where request-level behaviors (stream disconnects, server-side retry/fallback, and client retry with idempotency) caused double-counting or inconsistent attribution between (a) usage events and (b) the billing ledger. Unit/integration tests in the monorepo cover code paths, but we were missing an end-to-end canary that validates metering invariants against a live stack (gateway -> inference -> usage event emitter -> aggregation/export) under realistic failure modes.

This PR adds new perf-canary scenarios that intentionally trigger streaming + retry + fallback behaviors and then validate the expected metering invariants by querying the usage API / internal usage-events view within a bounded time window.

What’s included (new scenarios): 1) streaming_disconnect_no_double_count: Start a streaming chat completion, consume first N chunks, then terminate the client connection. Expectation: billed_tokens matches tokens actually generated before disconnect; no second usage event is emitted during cleanup; request_count increments exactly once; retry_count is 0. 2) server_retry_fallback_single_bill: Force a server-side retry/fallback by routing the request through a fault-injection profile (first attempt returns 5xx from upstream; second attempt succeeds). Expectation: the request is billed once; usage_event_id is stable across attempts (or deduped via attempt_group_id); raw_tokens may reflect both attempts in internal debug fields but billed_tokens reflects only the successful attempt; retry_count == 1. 3) client_retry_idempotent_key_dedup: Send a request with an Idempotency-Key, intentionally time out client-side before completion, retry with same key. Expectation: either (a) second call returns cached result and no additional billing occurs or (b) second call resumes and still produces a single billable event; duplicate_event_rate stays 0 for this request_id. 4) fallback_routing_region_attribution: Trigger a policy-based fallback from primary region to secondary region (capacity constraint simulation). Expectation: billing uses the final served region; region dimension is present and consistent across usage event + usage export; no “split” billing across regions for a single request.

Meter invariants validated: - Exactly-once billing per idempotency scope: sum(billed_tokens) and count(distinct billable_request_id) do not increase with retries/fallback beyond the expected single billable unit. - Streaming semantics: billed_tokens are derived from generated content that was successfully produced/committed, not from partial transport artifacts. - Attribution completeness: region, model, account_id, and endpoint are non-null; fallback routing uses the served_region dimension. - Dedupe signals: duplicate_event == false for all scenario request_ids; attempt_group_id groups retries.

Implementation notes: - Adds a small “meter_assert” helper that polls the usage API for the scenario request_ids with exponential backoff (default 8 minutes) to account for async event processing. - Uses deterministic scenario tags appended to metadata (x-redwood-canary-scenario + x-redwood-canary-run-id) so Finance/Eng can locate runs in Snowflake/Grafana during incident triage. - Adds fault-injection hooks via existing canary config (no new prod dependencies). - Adds a lightweight JSON snapshot written to canary artifacts (expected vs observed totals) for debugging and historical comparison.

Rollout/ops: - These scenarios are initially gated behind CANARY_ENABLE_METER_SCENARIOS=true in staging and canary-prod. - Alerting is set to “warn-only” for 1 week (no paging) until we establish baseline flakiness and tune the polling window.

Related: - redwood/pr-27463 (fix double-counting on stream retry) - redwood/pr-27518 (shadow billing compare mode) - redwood/pr-27485 (usage API: billed_tokens + cache_hit fields) - ENG-4430 (bug: prevent double-counting on retry path) - PM-1872 (metering change control / shadow period requirement)

Testing: - Local: make test (scenario unit tests) - Staging: ran canary suite x3 with fault injection enabled; no invariant violations; max usage-event lag observed 3m12s. - CI: lint + go test + docker build pass.

Notes for reviewers: Please focus on (1) invariant definitions (do we agree on billing semantics under streaming disconnect + retries), (2) polling/timeout defaults to avoid flake, and (3) scenario tagging fields to ensure they won’t leak to customer-visible metadata.

title: Add metering drift Grafana dashboard + alert rules (drift, freshness, duplicates)
preview:
Context
We’re standardizing a metering data-quality view so Finance/RevOps + Eng can quickly answer: (a) are usage events in sync with the billing ledger, (b) is data arriving on time for close/invoice previews, and (c) are we seeing duplicate usage events consistent with idempotency regressions/backfills.

This PR adds a Grafana dashboard (and initial alerting rules) for metering drift, freshness, and duplicate rates. It is intended to pair with the in-product Console sanity dashboard work (ENG-4415) and the scheduled drift job in the redwood monorepo (ENG-4421).

What’s included
- Dashboard: "Metering Sanity: Drift + Freshness" (folder: Billing/Metering)
  - Drift panels (usage_events vs billing_ledger aggregates)
    - Drift % by day (global)
    - Top-N accounts by absolute drift ($ and tokens) over last 24h / 7d
    - Drift by dimension: region, model, endpoint (chat/completions/embeddings)
    - Breakdown of drift causes when available (late_events, missing_dims, duplicates) from the drift job’s tagged metrics
  - Freshness panels
    - p50/p95 ingestion lag for usage_events (event_time -> available_in_warehouse)
    - Latest partition timestamps for key tables/exports (usage_events, usage_rollups, ledger_export)
    - "Freeze window" annotations (month-end close) based on the Finance agreed schedule
  - Duplicate rate panels
    - Duplicate usage_event_id rate (raw)
    - Duplicate dedupe_key collision rate (post-dedupe)
    - Retries/fallback signal overlays (when emitted) to correlate spikes

- Alerts (initial thresholds; tuned to avoid paging on small-account noise)
  - MeterDriftHigh (page): 24h drift % > 0.50% AND absolute drift > $2,000 OR > 5M billed_tokens, scoped per (region, endpoint)
  - MeterDriftModerate (ticket): 24h drift % > 0.25% AND absolute drift > $500
  - MeterFreshnessSLOBreach (page): p95 ingestion lag > 90 minutes for 30 minutes
  - MeterDuplicateRateHigh (page): duplicates > 0.10% of events for 20 minutes OR sustained > 0.05% for 2 hours
  - All alerts include runbook links + "known incident" annotation guidance

Metrics / data sources
- Prometheus series produced by drift job (redwood):
  - metering_drift_ratio{dimension=..., region=..., endpoint=...}
  - metering_drift_absolute_usd{...}
  - metering_drift_absolute_tokens{...}
  - metering_usage_ingestion_lag_seconds{quantile="0.95"}
  - metering_duplicate_rate{type="event_id"|"dedupe_key"}

Notes:
- This PR does not change metering logic. It only wires dashboard panels and rules to existing signals emitted by the job/export pipeline.
- For accounts with very small spend, percentage drift is noisy. Alerts gate on both % and absolute magnitude.

Rollout plan
1) Merge with alerts disabled by default (rule groups present; routing muted in prod for 48h).
2) Observe baseline noise during ongoing shadow billing compare.
3) Enable Moderate alerts to ticket first; then enable paging on DriftHigh + Freshness after threshold tuning.

Follow-ups
- Add a dedicated "embeddings batch" panel once ENG-4412 backfill completes (missing cache-hit/attribution fields).
- Add alert label for "meter_version" once shadow-billing compare exports meter_version consistently.

Screenshots
- Not included in PR (dashboards are generated from jsonnet). Use the rendered preview link in CI artifacts.

Refs
- Linear: ENG-4421 (alert on meter drift), ENG-4415 (Console dashboard)
- Slack threads: meter drift alert tuning, freshness SLO discussion
- Runbooks: Confluence runbook will be linked once published (placeholder link included).

title: Tiered SLO dashboards + deploy/incident annotations (Hosted vs Dedicated views)
preview: Summary: This PR adds a tiered SLO dashboard set to the observability-pack, including (1) Hosted SLO overview, (2) Dedicated SLO overview (tier-wide; no customer-level by default), and (3) a Dedicated vs Hosted comparison view. It also adds Grafana annotations for deploys and incidents so we can correlate latency spikes / streaming disconnects / 5xx bursts with changes and incident timelines. Motivation: Part of the Q1 reliability milestone to formalize tiered SLOs + error budgets, we need canonical, tier-aware views that match the new SLI tagging at ingress and avoid metric cardinality blowups. This PR is intended to be the “default” internal dashboard surface linked from burn-rate alerts and runbooks. Out of scope: customer-facing Dedicated per-account dashboards and access control (tracked in ENG-4946 follow-ups) and any changes to SLI definitions/recording rules (those land in the monorepo + telemetry repos). Dependencies/assumptions: Requires tier/deployment mode labels to be present on the primary request/latency/streaming metrics (see redwood PR-28411) and burn-rate recording rules to exist (redwood PR-28492). Key changes: (A) New dashboards: 1) SLO Overview — Hosted (tier=hosted, region filter, endpoint_class filter; panels for availability/success rate, p50/p95/p99 latency, streaming disconnect rate, and top contributors by endpoint_class + region). 2) SLO Overview — Dedicated (tier=dedicated; same core panels; includes headroom hints panel that links to fleet dashboards but does not embed per-customer dimensions). 3) SLO Compare — Hosted vs Dedicated (side-by-side availability, latency percentiles, and streaming reliability; uses a shared time range + shared region selection; intended for incident review and target negotiation with PM). (B) Annotation tracks: “Deploys” annotation fed by deploy event series (redwood_deploy_events_total + build SHA label, aggregated to avoid per-service noise), “Incidents” annotation fed by incident-bot exported events (incident_events_total) with severity and incident_id, and a “Budget burn state” annotation derived from burn-rate alert firing series for quick visual overlay. (C) Dashboard conventions: standard variables (region, endpoint_class, tier), consistent SLO links in panel descriptions, and a top-of-dashboard note on SLI exclusions (invalid requests, explicit rate limits, client aborts) to reduce confusion when support/PM teams compare dashboards with raw logs. (D) Cardinality guardrails: no per-model and no per-account breakdowns in the default dashboard; top contributors are capped to N=10 and use endpoint_class + route_group only (route_group is derived/normalized by recording rules). A hidden “__debug” toggle exists for SRE-only use to reveal model breakdown if the underlying metrics are safe; default is off. Validation: ran grafana-lint + jsonnet compilation; loaded dashboards into staging Grafana and verified variables resolve for us-east-1/eu-west-1; spot-checked that streaming disconnect panel does not include client aborts per current metric taxonomy. Screenshots: included in the PR conversation (staging links). Rollout: once merged, this pack is picked up by the weekly observability-pack sync job; SRE will pin alert runbook links to the new dashboard UIDs after deploy.

title: Add Suggestions API + funnel Grafana dashboards and beta alerting
preview: Summary: Adds a first-pass set of Grafana dashboards + alert rules for the Optimization Suggestions beta, covering (1) Suggestions API health (latency/errors/payload) and (2) the Suggestions view funnel (impression → expand → dismiss → apply-click), including breakdowns by org/model/route/region with conservative cardinality controls. Motivation: during dogfood we had a p95 regression + early customer reports of “empty despite traffic” and “estimates look off”; we need a single place for SRE/Platform + product to validate the pipeline (compute freshness), the API (error/latency), and the UX funnel (are people seeing/using suggestions). Scope notes: This PR only adds dashboards/alerts in observability-pack; it assumes the backend/UI already emits the metrics/events (ENG-19402) and the Suggestions API exists (ENG-19341). No changes to application code here. Dashboards added: (A) Optimize / Suggestions API Health: request rate, p50/p95 latency, 4xx/5xx, top error codes, payload size, cache hit/miss (if available), and per-route hot spots. Includes links to the runbook for triage. (B) Optimize / Suggestions Funnel (Beta): counts + conversion rates for suggestions_impression, suggestions_row_expand, suggestions_dismiss, suggestions_apply_click; time series + cohort table, with optional filters (org_id, project_id, model, route, region) and a “minimum volume” guardrail. (C) Optimize / Suggestions Compute Freshness: staleness/lag from the compute job (stale_as_of age), recompute success/failure, and lag percentiles so support can tell if “insufficient telemetry” is real vs pipeline delay. Alerting: Adds lightweight beta alert rules with conservative thresholds and paging only on clear system issues. SuggestionsAPIHigh5xxRate (page): 5xx > 1% for 10m (per env) with a minimum request volume gate. SuggestionsAPIP95LatencyHigh (page): p95 > 350ms for 15m (same volume gate). SuggestionsComputeLagHigh (page): compute freshness lag p95 > 6h for 30m. SuggestionsEventIngestionDrop (ticket): impressions drop to ~0 while API request volume is non-zero (helps catch client-side event pipeline breaks). All alerts include runbook links and labels for routing to eng-sre/eng-platform. Privacy/cardinality: Funnel breakdowns use org_id/project_id (numeric/UUID) and do not include org name; we cap table panels to top-N by volume and add a $__min_volume variable to reduce noisy / low-signal slices. Notes for rollout: Dashboards are safe to merge before the beta flag expands; alerts should be enabled in staging immediately and in prod with “ticket” severity for 24h before paging (see checklist).

(B) Optimize / Suggestions Funnel (Beta): counts + conversion rates for suggestions_impression, suggestions_row_expand, suggestions_dismiss, suggestions_apply_click; time series + cohort table, with optional filters (org_id, project_id, model, route, region) and a “minimum volume” guardrail. (C) Optimize / Suggestions Compute Freshness: staleness/lag from the compute job (stale_as_of age), recompute success/failure, and lag percentiles so support can tell if “insufficient telemetry” is real vs pipeline delay. Alerting: Adds lightweight beta alert rules with conservative thresholds and paging only on clear system issues. SuggestionsAPIHigh5xxRate (page): 5xx > 1% for 10m (per env) with a minimum request volume gate. SuggestionsAPIP95LatencyHigh (page): p95 > 350ms for 15m (same volume gate). SuggestionsComputeLagHigh (page): compute freshness lag p95 > 6h for 30m. SuggestionsEventIngestionDrop (ticket): impressions drop to ~0 while API request volume is non-zero (helps catch client-side event pipeline breaks). All alerts include runbook links and labels for routing to eng-sre/eng-platform. Privacy/cardinality: Funnel breakdowns use org_id/project_id (numeric/UUID) and do not include org name; we cap table panels to top-N by volume and add a $__min_volume variable to reduce noisy / low-signal slices. Notes for rollout: Dashboards are safe to merge before the beta flag expands; alerts should be enabled in staging immediately and in prod with “ticket” severity for 24h before paging (see checklist).

title: Audit log pipeline dashboards + alerts (ingestion latency, drop rate) with runbook links
preview:
Context: As part of the audit logging standardization effort we’re tightening operational visibility for the audit event ingestion/export path. We’ve had a couple of near-misses where ingestion lag spiked without a clear on-call landing page, and early alert drafts lacked enough context to triage quickly.

This PR adds:
- A Grafana dashboard for the audit-log ingestion pipeline (end-to-end delivery latency, ingest backlog, drop rate, retry/429/5xx, DLQ growth).
- Alert rules for delivery latency and drop rate with sane defaults + per-environment routing.
- Runbook links embedded in alert annotations and dashboard panel descriptions.

Key signals / definitions:
- delivery_latency_seconds: time from event emission at producer to ack at ingestion sink (measured via audit-ingest gateway metrics + exporter timestamps).
- drop_rate: fraction of events not successfully delivered after retry budget (from ingest and exporter counters).
- backlog: queued events in buffer (when enabled) + ingestion consumer lag.

Alert thresholds (initial):
- AuditLogDeliveryLatencyP99High: p99(delivery_latency_seconds) > 60s for 10m (page: enterprise tiers; ticket: others).
- AuditLogDropRateHigh: drop_rate > 0.10% for 5m (page; includes per-region breakdown).
- AuditLogIngestBacklogGrowing: backlog > 50k AND increasing for 15m (ticket; pages if > 200k).

Cardinality guardrails:
- Dashboard panels group primarily by region, deployment_mode (hosted|dedicated|private), and service. We intentionally do not group by raw tenant_id/project_id. event_type is only used in a single table panel with top-N + allow-listed types.

Runbooks:
- Incident runbook: sources/confluence/eng-sre/runbooks/audit-log-pipeline-incident-runbook.json
- Coverage regression runbook is linked from the dashboard “Coverage” row for follow-up when events are missing but pipeline is healthy.

Testing:
- jsonnetfmt + dashboards render in CI.
- promtool rules tests added for the new alert expressions.

Notes:
- This is intended to be the “default landing page” linked from paging alerts. We can iterate thresholds after a week of production data; see INT-3984 for noise tuning follow-ups.

title: Quality gates: dashboards + alert rules (pass rate, failure spikes, latency, flake rate)
preview: Adds first-class observability for Optimize quality gate runs. This PR introduces (1) a Grafana dashboard for the full gate-run funnel (created → running → passed/failed/overridden), (2) Prometheus alert rules for gate failure spikes, flake rate regressions, and gate execution latency/SLO breaches, and (3) runbook links + consistent labels so we can slice by config_type (batching/cache/quant_profile), model_family, route_id, account_tier, and region. Motivation: we’re rolling out mandatory gating ahead of applying Optimize recommendations; we need to catch (a) real quality regressions, (b) eval-harness instability/flaky suites causing false blocks, and (c) infrastructure constraints causing timeouts (notably EU-West). Design notes: alerts avoid paging on single-run failures; they trigger on burn-rate / sustained increases and include a ‘possible flake’ annotation when rerun aggregation indicates high variance. Dashboard includes deep links back into Console (gate run id) and the gate failure runbook for oncall. This aligns metric names/tags with the quality-gates observability standard and uses the new gate_run_id trace correlation field emitted by the Optimize service. Rollout: rules are initially deployed in ‘warning’ severity for 1 week; paging thresholds are enabled after we confirm baseline noise levels in limited production rollout.

title: Wire telemetry events for Suggestions funnel + server-side API usage logging
preview: Context: For the Optimize Suggestions beta, we need a closed-loop funnel to understand which recommendations are useful and where users drop off (impression → expand → dismiss → apply-click). This PR wires client-side analytics events from the Console Suggestions list + details drawer, and adds server-side logging/metrics on the /optimize/suggestions API for usage + debugging (without collecting request/response bodies). Scope: (1) UI events: suggestions_impression, suggestion_expand, suggestion_dismiss, suggestion_apply_click; (2) API logs + counters: optimize_suggestions.list requests, result counts, latency buckets, authz denied; (3) shared schema helpers to keep event props consistent across UI and backend. Non-goals: no PII, no raw prompts/responses, no customer-level savings numbers in analytics events (those remain in product surfaces only). Event properties (UI): org_id (hashed), project_id (hashed), route_id (stable), model_id, suggestion_id (stable), suggestion_type (batching|caching), risk_level (low|medium|high), confidence_bucket (low|med|high), rank_in_list, list_sort, filter_state, region, ui_surface (suggestions_table|details_drawer). Event properties (API): org_id (hashed), project_id (hashed), endpoint (/optimize/suggestions), http_status_class, authz_result (allow|deny), page_size, returned_count, stale_as_of_present (true|false), sort_key. Privacy: org/project identifiers are hashed at the client boundary before emission; suggestion_id is a stable ID already surfaced in the UI and does not contain tenant-identifying info. Sampling: impressions sampled at 20% by default (configurable) to reduce volume; expand/dismiss/apply-click are unsampled. Rollout: behind feature flag optimize_suggestions_beta; events only emit when the flag is enabled and the Suggestions page is rendered. Testing: added unit tests for schema normalization, and Playwright coverage for emitting expand/dismiss/apply-click from the drawer. Related: aligns with dashboards work in observability-pack (PR #1843) and supports the beta funnel dashboard spec.

title: Post error budget burn summaries to #eng-sre on burn-rate alerts (dashboards + runbook links)
preview:
Context: As part of the tiered SLO + error budget program, we’re standardizing burn-rate alerting and enforcement. Today the burn-rate alerts page the on-call, but triage is still slow because the initial signal doesn’t include (a) how fast we’re burning, (b) what’s driving it (latency vs errors vs streaming disconnects), or (c) where to go next (dashboards/runbooks). This PR updates incident-bot to listen for burn-rate alert notifications and post a structured “burn summary” message into #eng-sre (and thread replies for updates), including deep links to the tiered SLO dashboards and the active mitigation runbook.

What this does: 1) Adds a new alert type handler for error budget burn-rate alerts (Prometheus Alertmanager payloads via our existing webhook). 2) Builds a canonical Slack message format: header with SLO name + tier + region, a short diagnosis (burn rate + window + budget remaining), and a set of links (Grafana dashboard, logs/traces, runbook). 3) Adds a lightweight “top contributors” section when the alert includes a `route` or `endpoint_class` label (to avoid cardinality explosion we only surface what’s already in the alert labels). 4) Dedupe/ratelimit: repeated firings for the same SLO/tier/region within 15 minutes update an existing thread instead of spamming the channel. 5) Safety: scrub potentially sensitive labels (customer/account identifiers) and only allow a safe-label allowlist to render in Slack.

Message contents (example): - Title: “Error budget burning: hosted / us-east-1 / chat.completions (p95 latency)” - Summary line: “Burn rate: 6.2x (2h window), budget remaining (30d): 62% → projected exhaustion in ~4.8d if sustained.” - Links: “Tiered SLO dashboard”, “Regional comparison”, “Runbook: error budget breach mitigation”, “Incident process (when to declare)”. - Footer: build/version and alert fingerprint.

Config: - Adds `BURN_SUMMARY_CHANNEL` (default #eng-sre in prod) and `BURN_SUMMARY_ENABLED` feature flag (default false). - Adds `GRAFANA_BASE_URL` and `GRAFANA_DASHBOARD_UID_TIERED_SLO` for generating direct links with pre-filled variables (tier/region/endpoint_class). - Adds `RUNBOOK_URL_ERROR_BUDGET_BREACH` to keep the link stable as Confluence pages move.

Rollout plan: - Stage: enable in warn-only mode (`BURN_SUMMARY_ENABLED=true`, channel #eng-sre-sandbox) for 1 week; validate formatting + dedupe behavior. - Prod: enable to #eng-sre after signoff from SRE on-call and CS escalation owners. - Follow-up: once stable, add an @-mention for the correct rotation based on tier (Dedicated vs Hosted) and severity mapping.

Out of scope: - Computing burn-rate itself (handled by recording rules + alerts in redwood/observability-pack). - Any new per-customer metrics; we explicitly avoid printing customer/account identifiers.

Refs: - Related alerting work: redwood/pr-28492-add-burn-rate-alerting-queries-and-recording-rules.json - Related dashboards: observability-pack/pr-6312-tiered-slo-dashboards-and-annotations.json - Runbook: confluence/eng-platform/runbooks/error-budget-breach-mitigation-runbook.json

title: Add SDK retry/timeout/streaming disconnect dashboard panels + rollout alerts
preview:
Context: As part of the cross-language SDK parity rollout (standard retries/backoff, timeout model, and streaming disconnect handling), SRE requested first-class dashboards and conservative alerts to monitor load impact and customer-facing failure rates during beta/stable releases. This PR adds (1) new panels to the existing "SDK Client Health" Grafana dashboard for retry rate, timeout rate, and streaming disconnect reasons, (2) companion alert rules with rollout-friendly thresholds and burn-rate style paging, and (3) a small set of recording rules to make the queries cheaper/consistent across environments.

What’s included: - Dashboard panels: Retry rate (by sdk_language, endpoint, model, status_code), Retry attempts distribution (p50/p95 of retry_count per request), Timeout rate (by sdk_language, timeout_scope=overall|connect|read), Timeout duration (histogram heatmap where available), Streaming disconnect rate (by sdk_language, endpoint, reason), Top streaming disconnect reasons (table), and a rollout view grouped by sdk_version (when the label is present). - Alerts (disabled-by-default in prod, enabled in beta + dogfood): (A) High SDK retry rate (warn), (B) High SDK timeout rate (warn), (C) Streaming disconnect spike (warn), (D) Potential retry storm indicator (page) using (retry_total / request_total) + 429 rate + concurrency as gates. - Recording rules: pre-aggregate sdk_client_request_total, sdk_client_retry_total, sdk_client_timeout_total, sdk_client_stream_disconnect_total into 5m rate series with a bounded label set.

Metrics used (existing / recently standardized fields): - sdk_client_request_total{sdk_language, sdk_version?, endpoint, model, status_code} - sdk_client_retry_total{sdk_language, endpoint, reason} - sdk_client_timeout_total{sdk_language, endpoint, timeout_scope} - sdk_client_stream_disconnect_total{sdk_language, endpoint, reason} - sdk_client_request_duration_seconds_bucket (optional; used when emitted)

Notes / assumptions: - Streaming disconnect reasons follow the parity taxonomy being rolled out in SDKs (e.g., server_fin, read_timeout, client_cancel, proxy_reset, tls_error, unknown). Panels will still render if reason is missing (falls into reason="unknown" in the dashboards). - To avoid alert noise from a small number of clients, alerts include a minimum volume gate (requests_per_min) and are scoped to the rollout dashboards folder. - This PR intentionally does NOT add new instrumentation; it only consumes the standardized fields being added in the SDK repos. If a given SDK release doesn’t yet include sdk_version label, the dashboard will still work (the sdk_version view will simply be empty).

Rollout plan: - Merge now; ship to dogfood + beta monitoring stacks. - Enable alerts in beta immediately; keep prod alerts muted until stable releases are out (target: after ENG-5131 go/no-go). - During the first week of stable: watch retry_rate, timeout_rate, and disconnect_reason mix for proxy_reset/read_timeout regressions; use request_id sampling from SDK logs to correlate.

Testing: - Rendered dashboards via jsonnet/grafonnet compile locally; validated queries against staging Prometheus with synthetic load. - Ran alert rule unit tests (promtool) with fixtures covering 429 spikes and elevated read timeouts.

Screenshots: Included in the PR conversation (Grafana panels: Retry rate by language, Streaming disconnect reasons table).

Follow-ups: - Add a dedicated panel for Retry-After compliance once the gateway header adoption is >90% (separate PR). - Consider a dashboard annotation hook for SDK release cut times (Connor coordinating in ENG-RELEASES).

title: perf-canary: add paging (Sev2/Sev1) alert rules for sustained per-model regressions
preview: Summary: This adds paging-tier perf-canary alert rules (Sev2 + Sev1) for sustained regressions on top models. These are intentionally stricter than the warning rules in pr-19179 (higher minimum sample size, longer sustain window, and stricter anti-flap) and route to the on-call rotation channel via incident-bot. Motivation: During phase-1 rollout we saw warning-level regressions that were actionable when they sustained (real tail-latency impact), but the existing rules were designed to be noisy-tolerant and non-paging. This PR implements the paging path that SRE approved in the paging policy thread and is required for rollout gates integration. Alerting model: We page only for top-model + sustained regressions where we have enough canary volume to reduce false positives, and we gate on a canary-health signal (telemetry freshness + request loop healthy) to avoid paging on broken canary. Key behaviors: 1) Severity levels: Sev2 (page) for sustained p95 latency regression OR sustained tokens/sec drop on top models; Sev1 (page) only if regression is severe and sustained across consecutive windows. 2) Attribution-friendly annotations: every alert includes model, model_version, runtime_build, kernel_tag (if present), region, hardware_sku, baseline id, observed vs baseline deltas, and deep links to the Grafana dashboard + runbook. 3) Anti-flap + dedupe: minimum sample size, sustain windows, and incident-bot dedupe keys based on (model, region, hardware_sku, signal, severity). 4) Routing: Sev2/Sev1 route to #eng-oncall and create an incident-bot thread tagged perf-canary; warning-level continues to #eng-runtime. Thresholds (initial defaults, tuned for us-east phase-1): p95_e2e_latency_ms regression: Sev2 if >= +15% over baseline for 20m AND samples >= 200 AND canary_health=ok; Sev1 if >= +30% for 30m AND samples >= 300 AND canary_health=ok. tokens_per_sec regression: Sev2 if <= -12% for 25m AND samples >= 150; Sev1 if <= -20% for 35m AND samples >= 250. Note: These are defaults; per-model overrides live in control-plane config and will be adjusted during threshold calibration. Rollout: These rules are initially scoped to top models only (via label matcher on canary_model_tier=top). They are disabled by default in non-prod; in prod they are deployed but will be set to "dry-run" (notify-only) for 48h in us-east until SRE sign-off, then flipped to paging. Testing/validation: - Unit: alert rule lint + promtool rules tests; - Integration: rendered rules in staging Grafana; - Manual: replayed recent canary time series (including known regression from SUP-3841) to confirm expected firing behavior and no false triggers on short spikes. Links: - Linear ENG-4878 (alerting policy + routing), ENG-4880 (phase-1 us-east rollout), PM-642 (rollout gates integration) - Runbook: confluence/eng-sre/runbooks/runbook-perf-regression-canary-alert - Dashboard: grafana/perf-canary/per-model (added in pr-19174) - Prior art: pr-19179 (warning-level rules) Checklist: - [x] rules have sustain window + min sample size - [x] rules gated by canary health and telemetry freshness - [x] incident-bot routing + dedupe key added - [x] annotations include dashboard/runbook links - [x] promtool tests added - [x] reviewed by SRE for paging hygiene

Key behaviors: 1) Severity levels: Sev2 (page) for sustained p95 latency regression OR sustained tokens/sec drop on top models; Sev1 (page) only if regression is severe and sustained across consecutive windows. 2) Attribution-friendly annotations: every alert includes model, model_version, runtime_build, kernel_tag (if present), region, hardware_sku, baseline id, observed vs baseline deltas, and deep links to the Grafana dashboard + runbook. 3) Anti-flap + dedupe: minimum sample size, sustain windows, and incident-bot dedupe keys based on (model, region, hardware_sku, signal, severity). 4) Routing: Sev2/Sev1 route to #eng-oncall and create an incident-bot thread tagged perf-canary; warning-level continues to #eng-runtime. Thresholds (initial defaults, tuned for us-east phase-1): p95_e2e_latency_ms regression: Sev2 if >= +15% over baseline for 20m AND samples >= 200 AND canary_health=ok; Sev1 if >= +30% for 30m AND samples >= 300 AND canary_health=ok. tokens_per_sec regression: Sev2 if <= -12% for 25m AND samples >= 150; Sev1 if <= -20% for 35m AND samples >= 250. Note: These are defaults; per-model overrides live in control-plane config and will be adjusted during threshold calibration. Rollout: These rules are initially scoped to top models only (via label matcher on canary_model_tier=top). They are disabled by default in non-prod; in prod they are deployed but will be set to "dry-run" (notify-only) for 48h in us-east until SRE sign-off, then flipped to paging. Testing/validation: - Unit: alert rule lint + promtool rules tests; - Integration: rendered rules in staging Grafana; - Manual: replayed recent canary time series (including known regression from SUP-3841) to confirm expected firing behavior and no false triggers on short spikes. Links: - Linear ENG-4878 (alerting policy + routing), ENG-4880 (phase-1 us-east rollout), PM-642 (rollout gates integration) - Runbook: confluence/eng-sre/runbooks/runbook-perf-regression-canary-alert - Dashboard: grafana/perf-canary/per-model (added in pr-19174) - Prior art: pr-19179 (warning-level rules) Checklist: - [x] rules have sustain window + min sample size - [x] rules gated by canary health and telemetry freshness - [x] incident-bot routing + dedupe key added - [x] annotations include dashboard/runbook links - [x] promtool tests added - [x] reviewed by SRE for paging hygiene

title: Add Usage Insights freshness/quality dashboard + alerts (lag + reconciliation drift)
preview:
Motivation: Usage Insights is going into beta with interactive console queries backed by hourly/day partitions and reconciliation checks against billing. We need a standard dashboard + alert bundle in observability-pack so SRE/on-call can quickly answer: (1) is data fresh, (2) are we dropping/late-ingesting events by route/model, (3) are we drifting vs billing totals beyond tolerance, and (4) are query endpoints timing out for common ranges.

This PR adds a Grafana dashboard and alert rule templates for freshness lag, late event rate, reconciliation drift, and query p95 latency. The dashboard is designed to be imported into customer-facing/internal stacks with minimal per-cluster edits (templated variables for env/region/deployment_mode/workspace_id).

What’s included: 1) Dashboard: “Usage Insights — Data Freshness & Quality” (Grafana JSON) Panels: Aggregation lag (p50/p95) by dataset (route_model_hourly, route_model_daily) and by region; Ingestion late-event ratio (late_events / total_events) by region + streaming endpoint flag; Missing route_tag rate (events where route_template = "unknown" or null); Reconciliation drift (%) between insights totals and billing hook totals, per workspace and overall; Top offenders table: workspaces with highest drift and highest lag; Query API p95 latency + error rate (v1/usage-insights/* endpoints) with annotations for deploys.

2) Alerts (PrometheusRule templates): - UsageInsightsAggregationLagHigh: fires when p95 aggregation lag > 20m for 15m (warn) and > 60m for 10m (page). - UsageInsightsLateEventRatioHigh: fires when late_event_ratio > 2% for 30m (warn), with a separate page threshold at 5% for 15m. - UsageInsightsRouteTagMissing: fires when missing_route_tag_rate > 1% for 30m (warn) and > 3% for 15m (page). - UsageInsightsReconciliationDriftHigh: fires when abs(drift_pct) > 0.5% for 2h (warn) and > 1.0% for 1h (page), excluding small totals (< $50/day) to reduce noise. - UsageInsightsQueryP95Degraded: fires when p95 > 2500ms for 15m (warn) and > 5000ms for 10m (page), scoped to the insights query service.

3) Runbook stub: Adds a runbook page with investigation steps and links to relevant services/datasets (warehouse partitions, ingestion pipeline, billing hook job).

Metric assumptions (coordinated with Telemetry/Analytics): This PR uses the metric names already in-flight on redwood (see ENG-3824/ENG-3817): usage_insights_aggregation_lag_seconds{dataset,region,deployment_mode}; usage_insights_late_event_ratio{region,streaming}; usage_insights_missing_route_tag_rate{region}; usage_insights_reconciliation_drift_pct{workspace_id}; usage_insights_query_request_duration_ms_bucket{route,status}; usage_insights_query_errors_total{route,status}. If any naming changes land before beta, we can follow up with a patch release of observability-pack.

Noise/guardrails: The drift alert includes a minimum spend floor and a 2h lookback to avoid paging on transient rounding/timing skew (e.g., hourly partitions catching up). The lag alert pages only on sustained lag > 60m.

Testing/validation: Imported dashboard into staging Grafana; validated queries on canary metrics in us-east-1 and eu-west-1. Used replayed lag/drift fixtures from staging to verify alert routing and labels.

Rollout notes: This is safe to ship ahead of beta. SREs should import the dashboard to the internal "Console & Tooling" folder and ensure Alertmanager routes page severity=page to #eng-sre, and warn severity=warn to #eng-platform.

Screenshots: (not included in PR; Grafana JSON is the source of truth).

title: Add Shadow Traffic quality delta dashboards + scoring lag panels (cardinality-safe)
preview:
## Summary
This PR adds a first-cut set of Grafana dashboards to visualize Shadow Traffic quality deltas (control vs shadow variants) and scoring pipeline lag/health. The dashboards are designed for Hosted/Dedicated/Private and intentionally avoid high-cardinality breakdowns (no prompt_id, request_fingerprint, raw route_id, customer_id).

This is intended to support beta oncall readiness and help triage the two main failure classes seen during dogfood/beta:
1) quality deltas missing/stale due to scoring lag or ingestion lag
2) metrics backend pressure due to accidental high-cardinality dimensions

## What’s included
- New "Shadow Traffic / Quality Deltas" dashboard:
  - Δ quality score (shadow - control) over time with confidence bands (when emitted)
  - refusal rate delta, format adherence delta
  - sample size panels (n_control, n_shadow) + effective sample rate
  - breakdowns limited to:
    - deployment_mode (hosted|dedicated|private)
    - model_family (e.g., llama3, qwen2, mistral)
    - route_group (sanitized bucket: "core_chat", "embeddings", "tools", etc.)
    - rubric_version (pinned)
    - experiment_id (capped / top-k only)

- New "Shadow Traffic / Scoring Lag & Worker Health" dashboard:
  - scoring queue lag (p50/p95/p99) + oldest message age
  - worker throughput, retry rate, DLQ count
  - ingestion lag for shadow_pair events
  - circuit breaker open rate + dropped shadow attempts (fail-open)

- Alert rule proposals (NOT enabled by default):
  - scoring pipeline lag SLO breach (ticket -> page escalation guidance in annotations)
  - missing scoring signal (no scored pairs for X minutes while shadow attempts > 0)
  - quality regression advisory alert (requires minimum sample size + CI)

## Cardinality / safety notes
This PR follows the cardinality constraints discussed in SUP-28510 and the Shadow Metrics ADR:
- No dashboards query by prompt_id/request_fingerprint.
- experiment_id panels use a top-k query and default to aggregate view.
- route_id is replaced with route_group generated upstream (low-cardinality mapping maintained by telemetry).
- model_id is avoided; we use model_family + (optional) variant_role (control|shadow).

## Files changed (high level)
- dashboards/shadow_traffic_quality_deltas.jsonnet
- dashboards/shadow_scoring_lag_and_health.jsonnet
- dashboards/_library/shadow_traffic_panels.libsonnet
- alerts/shadow_scoring_lag_rules.yml (proposed)
- alerts/shadow_quality_regression_rules.yml (proposed)
- dashboards/README.md (usage + label guidance)

## Example panel queries (illustrative)
- Quality delta (aggregate):
  sum(rate(redwood_shadow_quality_score_sum{deployment_mode=~"$deployment_mode", rubric_version=~"$rubric_version"}[5m])) / sum(rate(redwood_shadow_quality_score_count{deployment_mode=~"$deployment_mode", rubric_version=~"$rubric_version"}[5m]))
  -
  sum(rate(redwood_control_quality_score_sum{deployment_mode=~"$deployment_mode", rubric_version=~"$rubric_version"}[5m])) / sum(rate(redwood_control_quality_score_count{deployment_mode=~"$deployment_mode", rubric_version=~"$rubric_version"}[5m]))

- Scoring lag (p95):
  histogram_quantile(0.95, sum by (le, deployment_mode) (rate(redwood_shadow_scoring_lag_seconds_bucket{deployment_mode=~"$deployment_mode"}[5m])))

## Rollout plan
- Merge to observability-pack, dashboards deployed to internal Grafana first (dogfood tenants)
- Enable for Dedicated beta tenants once telemetry labels are confirmed stable (route_group + model_family present)
- Alert rules remain "proposed" until SRE signs off on thresholds and paging policy

## Screenshots
Not included in PR description (dashboards are generated in CI and reviewed via preview JSON in artifacts). See CI artifact: grafana-dashboards-preview.zip

## Checklist
- [x] Panels avoid high-cardinality dimensions
- [x] Default views are aggregate-first; breakdowns are opt-in
- [x] Added dashboard annotations linking to runbook sections (shadow scoring lag / missing data)
- [x] CI renders jsonnet and validates Grafana schema

## Notes / follow-ups
- Follow-up: add a small "label sanitizer" doc with examples of safe/unsafe breakdowns (Logan/Nadia request)
- Follow-up: once confidence interval emission is standardized, switch CI panel to render CI bands instead of placeholder series

Related:
- ENG-5409 (alerting quality regression detection)
- ENG-5410 (cardinality guardrails for shadow metrics)
- SUP-28477 (missing quality dashboard data for Dedicated tenant)
- SUP-28510 (cardinality alerts after enabling shadow traffic)

title: Policy engine: emit v1 audit events for routing policy + fallback updates (diff + cardinality checks)
preview:
Context
We’re standardizing audit logging coverage for sensitive actions across Hosted/Dedicated/Private. Routing policy changes (incl. fallback rules) are security-relevant because they can alter what models serve which requests, potentially impacting data handling boundaries and reliability controls.

This PR instruments the policy engine to emit schema v1 audit events for routing policy CRUD + publish operations, with:
- A bounded diff generator (before/after) to support investigations
- Cardinality guards to prevent high-cardinality payloads from polluting downstream SIEMs
- Consistent actor/target semantics + correlation IDs per the v1 standard

What’s included
- Audit events emitted for:
  - Routing policy create/update/delete
  - Fallback configuration update (primary/fallback model sets, failure-mode rules)
  - Allow/deny list changes for model routing
  - Policy publish/activate (when a draft becomes effective)
- New event types (registered):
  - policy.routing.created
  - policy.routing.updated
  - policy.routing.deleted
  - policy.routing.published
  - policy.fallback.updated
- Diff summary generation:
  - Produces a stable, bounded summary string + structured keys for a small allow-list of fields
  - Drops/obfuscates unbounded data (e.g., free-form match expressions) and emits a reason tag (diff_truncated=true, truncated_fields=[...])
  - For lists (e.g., model allow-lists), emits added/removed counts and a capped sample (max 10) of normalized IDs
- Cardinality checks:
  - Enforces allow-listing for attribute keys on the audit event extension payload (policy.* namespace)
  - Normalizes/caps values: strings max 256 chars, lists max 50 entries, emits *_count fields for full sizes
  - Rejects emission only for the extension payload (event still emitted with minimal required fields) and logs an internal metric audit_extension_dropped_total{reason=...}
- Correlation + attribution:
  - request_id + trace_id propagated from gateway -> policy service
  - actor derived from auth context (user/service principal); supports impersonation context when present
  - source_ip captured when available (gateway forwarded header), otherwise omitted (not synthesized)

Files / components touched (high level)
- services/policy-engine
  - routing_policy_service.go: emit events on write paths
  - diff/routing_policy_diff.go: allow-listed diff generation + truncation
  - audit/policy_audit_emitter.go: helper to build v1 events + extension validation
- libs/audit
  - event_types_registry.ts: register new event types + required fields
  - cardinality/guards.ts: namespace allow-list + value caps
- tests
  - unit tests for diff (order-insensitive list behavior, truncation, cap enforcement)
  - integration test for routing policy update emitting policy.routing.updated with expected fields

Example emitted event (sanitized)
- event_type: policy.routing.updated
- action: update
- actor_type: user
- actor_id: usr_01HT…
- resource_type: routing_policy
- resource_id: rp_7f3a…
- scope: tenant
- schema_version: 1.0
- extension.policy.diff_summary: "fallback_mode: 'region' -> 'model_variant'; allow_list_models: +2/-1 (sample: [mdl_llama3_70b_q4, mdl_llama3_70b_fp16])"
- extension.policy.diff_truncated: false

Design notes / trade-offs
- We intentionally avoid embedding raw match expressions or full JSON policy documents in audit logs (PII risk + cardinality). Instead we emit:
  - A bounded diff summary
  - Counts + small samples of normalized IDs
  - A pointer to the policy_id/version to fetch the full policy from the control plane if the investigator has authorization
- Event delivery is best-effort for this service (aligned with ADR in progress), but we emit metrics on failures and do not block policy writes on audit sink issues.

Testing
- go test ./services/policy-engine/... (new diff + emitter unit tests)
- Integration: TestRoutingPolicyUpdateEmitsAuditEvent (uses audit test sink)
- Lint: event_type registry enforcement (fails build on unregistered types)

Rollout
- Behind feature flag: AUDIT_V1_POLICY_ENGINE_EVENTS (default on in staging, can be enabled per-environment)
- Observability: new metrics
  - audit_events_emitted_total{event_type=...}
  - audit_events_failed_total{reason=...}
  - audit_extension_dropped_total{reason=...}

Links
- Linear: ENG-4838 (policy engine audit events)
- Related: SUP-18477 (customer reports inconsistent fields in SIEM exports)

Review notes
- Please focus on: diff allow-list correctness, cardinality caps, and whether the event types/fields match the taxonomy we’re converging on.

---
Conversation / review timeline
2026-01-29 (Allison Grant): "Diff logic looks good, but please ensure truncation is explicit to the consumer. Also the sample list must be deterministic (sort) to avoid noisy SIEM diffs." 
2026-01-29 (Jordan Lee): "Ack. Added diff_truncated + truncated_fields and made model ID sampling sorted after normalization. Updated tests for determinism."

2026-01-30 (Ethan Park): "We should include policy_version in the event so investigators can correlate which revision was applied. Also: do we emit for publish vs update?" 
2026-01-30 (Jordan Lee): "Added resource_version (policy_version) and a separate policy.routing.published event emitted only on activate. Update continues to fire for draft edits." 

2026-02-01 (Marcus Lin): "Cardinality guard behavior: do not drop the entire event if extension fails validation; keep minimal event and record a metric. Also check source_ip handling (don’t trust arbitrary headers)." 
2026-02-01 (Jordan Lee): "Implemented ‘extension drop’ path: event always emitted with required fields; extension omitted on validation error; metric increments with reason. source_ip now only populated from gateway-provided verified context (not raw header) with fallback to empty." 

2026-02-03 (Logan Wright): "Can we add a small integration test assertion for correlation IDs? We’ve had gaps in request_id propagation." 
2026-02-03 (Jordan Lee): "Added assertion for request_id + trace_id in integration test when the test harness includes a trace context." 

CI
- Initial run failed (2026-02-02): lint rejected unregistered event type policy.fallback.updated (registry update was in separate commit) 
- Rerun after registry fix: all checks green

Final approvals
- Allison Grant: Approved (2026-02-04)
- Ethan Park: Approved (2026-02-04)
- Marcus Lin: Approved (2026-02-04)

Merge
Squash merged to main on 2026-02-04. Flag enabled in staging; production rollout tracked in ENG-4838 checklist.

title: perf-canary: add warning-level alert rules (dampening + min sample size) routed to #eng-runtime
preview:
## Summary
Adds the initial set of warning-level perf-canary alerts (no paging) to detect per-model serving performance regressions early, with explicit guardrails to reduce noise during ramp.

This PR wires:
- Warning alerts for p95 latency regression and tokens/sec drops
- Dampening (sustained windows) + min-sample conditions
- Alertmanager routing to Slack #eng-runtime
- Runbook + dashboard links in annotations

This is intended to be enabled first in us-east with a limited model list (per ENG-4880 rollout plan). Paging rules are tracked separately (pr-19183).

## Motivation / context
During early perf-canary rollout we saw false positives driven by low volume, baseline drift, and transient telemetry delays. The warning rules in this PR aim to be:
- Low-noise: only fire when there’s enough data
- Actionable: include baseline vs observed deltas + direct links
- Safe: warning-only channel routing while we calibrate

## Alerting approach (v0)
### What we alert on
1) **p95 end-to-end latency regression** (primary): observed p95 vs baseline p95
2) **tokens/sec drop** (secondary): observed tokens/sec vs baseline tokens/sec

### Key guardrails
- **Minimum sample size**: require >= N completed canary requests in the evaluation window
- **Dampening**: require condition to hold for a continuous duration before firing
- **Cardinality limits**: alert labels only include model + region + tier + runtime_build_id (no prompt-shape label)

### Windows / thresholds (v0 defaults)
- p95 latency regression: >= +12% for 15m (warning)
- tokens/sec drop: <= -10% for 15m (warning)
- min samples: >= 200 requests in 15m per (model, region, tier)

These defaults are intentionally conservative; per-model overrides will be managed via the baseline/threshold config in the perf-canary service and/or follow-up PR if we need rule-level overrides.

## Implementation details
- Adds alerting rules under `alerts/perf_canary/warnings.yaml`
- Adds an Alertmanager route/receiver for `slack_eng_runtime` with a grouping key on (alertname, model, region, tier)
- Adds templates used to render compact Slack messages including baseline/observed/threshold and deep links

### Example rule snippet
The core pattern is:

- Ensure sufficient volume:
  `sum(increase(perf_canary_requests_total{status="ok"}[15m])) by (model, region, tier) >= 200`

- Compare observed p95 to baseline p95 (both expressed in ms):
  `histogram_quantile(0.95, sum(rate(perf_canary_e2e_latency_ms_bucket[5m])) by (le, model, region, tier)) / on(model, region, tier) group_left perf_canary_baseline_p95_latency_ms`

- Fire when ratio exceeds threshold for long enough:
  `> 1.12 for: 15m`

Annotations include:
- `runbook_url`: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/SRE/Runbook%3A+Perf+Regression+Canary+Alert
- `dashboard_url`: https://grafana.redwoodinference.com/d/perf-canary/perf-canary?var-model={{ $labels.model }}&var-region={{ $labels.region }}

## Testing
- `promtool check rules` in CI
- `promtool test rules` added/updated for:
  - happy path: regression sustained with min samples -> fires
  - low-volume path: regression but samples < min -> does not fire
  - transient spike: regression for < for-window -> does not fire
- Alertmanager config validation

## Rollout plan
1) Merge rules disabled by default (gated by `perf_canary_alerts_enabled` config in the alerting pipeline)
2) Enable in us-east for 2–3 models with only warning routing
3) Tune thresholds/min-samples if needed (ENG-4882)
4) Add paging rules separately once warning noise is acceptable (pr-19183)

## Risks / mitigations
- Risk: telemetry ingestion delay causes late datapoints and apparent regressions
  - Mitigation: long `for:` window + min samples; dashboard links include ingestion-lag panel (from pr-19174)
- Risk: baseline drift causes slow burn alerts
  - Mitigation: warning-only; follow-up ADR work for baseline update policy

## Screenshots
N/A (rule-based); see CI output for promtool rule tests.

## Checklist
- [x] Warning alerts only (no paging)
- [x] Min sample size guardrails
- [x] Dampening windows
- [x] Slack routing to #eng-runtime
- [x] Runbook + dashboard links
- [x] promtool validation + tests

Related:
- Dashboards: pr-19174
- Paging rules: pr-19183
- Perf-canary payload formatting: perf-canary pr-19244

title: Add baseline fetch + regression evaluator (min samples, rolling window, pct thresholds)
preview:
Implements the baseline lookup path and the first pass of regression evaluation logic for perf-canary. This is the missing piece between “we can generate load + emit raw latency/tps metrics” and “we can decide whether something is actually regressing (and do so with low noise)”. The evaluator is intentionally conservative for phase 1: it enforces minimum sample sizes, uses a rolling window over recent canary runs, and compares observed vs baseline using percent-change thresholds per metric.

Context / why: We’ve seen early canary runs spike due to batch composition variance and telemetry ingestion jitter. We need a consistent evaluation step that (a) won’t flap on small sample sizes, (b) is explicit about confidence, and (c) produces a stable verdict we can wire into alert rules + rollout gates.

What’s included:
- Baseline client: fetches baseline artifacts from the Baseline Store service (read-only) using a stable key.
- Baseline keying: model_id + model_version + runtime_build + hardware_sku (+ region when configured). The default is region-agnostic to avoid exploding baseline count; region-specific is opt-in per model via config.
- Regression evaluator:
  - Requires min_samples per metric (default 200 requests per window; configurable).
  - Rolling evaluation window: N most recent intervals (default 10m window with 1m buckets; configurable). Buckets are aggregated using trimmed mean for rate metrics and quantile merge for latency histograms.
  - Thresholds are percent-change relative to baseline, expressed per metric as {warn, page} (page is not used by this repo yet but is emitted in verdict payload for downstream alerting).
  - Anti-flap: uses “M-of-K” rule for verdict state transitions (default 3 of last 5 windows must violate threshold) and a cooldown period before clearing (default 15m) to avoid oscillation.
- Missing/invalid baseline handling: if baseline is absent, stale, or incompatible (e.g., schema mismatch), we emit a “NO_BASELINE” verdict and skip regression evaluation (still emits raw metrics). This is intended to be safe during early rollout.
- Output: emits a structured regression verdict (JSON log event + metrics) containing baseline stats, observed stats, computed deltas, and a confidence score. (Alert webhook payload formatting is handled in a follow-up PR.)

Metric coverage (phase 1):
- e2e_latency_ms (p50/p95/p99)
- runtime_latency_ms (p50/p95/p99) when runtime instrumentation is present
- tokens_per_sec
- queueing_time_ms (if present; treated as attribution signal rather than primary regression trigger)

Notes / non-goals:
- No baseline writing here (owned by baseline generator job / pipeline).
- No customer prompt shadowing. Canary inputs remain synthetic.
- Not attempting “auto-baseline drift correction” in this PR; we only validate staleness via baseline metadata + a max_age guard.

Config additions:
- baseline:
  - endpoint (required)
  - request_timeout_ms
  - max_age_hours (default 168)
  - include_region_in_key (default false; can be overridden per model)
- evaluator:
  - window_minutes, bucket_minutes
  - min_samples
  - violation_m_of_k, violation_k
  - clear_cooldown_minutes
  - thresholds: per-metric warn/page pct deltas

Testing:
- Unit tests for key construction + baseline deserialization.
- Golden tests for evaluator verdicts across common cases: below threshold, above threshold, too few samples, missing baseline, mixed metrics.
- Property-ish test for anti-flap (verdict only flips after M-of-K is satisfied).

Rollout plan:
- Run in observe-only mode for us-east for 2–3 models; validate that verdicts match expectation against staging baselines.
- After 1 week of stability, enable warning-level alert rules in observability-pack.

Screenshots / examples:
- Example verdict log line is included in docs/perf-canary/evaluator.md (shows per-metric deltas + confidence).

Refs:
- Linear: ENG-4874 (baseline store + versioning), ENG-4873 (metric defs)
- Upcoming: pr-19244 (alert webhook payload), observability-pack alert rules PRs

Operational concerns:
- Baseline fetch is cached in-process with a TTL (default 10m) to avoid hammering the store.
- All emitted labels are cardinality-reviewed: model_id, model_version, runtime_build, hardware_sku, region (optional). No request IDs, no prompt hashes.

Requested reviewer focus:
- Nadia/Logan: baseline keying + staleness/compat semantics
- Sofia: rolling window choices + anti-flap logic (do we have enough dampening?)
- Allison: signal naming and whether the output shape is sufficient for downstream alerting/runbooks

title: Add Optimize suggestions API contract (schema + pagination/sorting)
preview: Context: Console is adding a new Suggestions view (beta) under Optimize. This PR introduces the backend API contract the Console will consume: endpoint shape, response schema for savings + risk, and server-side pagination/sorting. Scope is intentionally contract + plumbing only; RBAC enforcement and the compute job that populates suggestions land in follow-ups (ENG-19377 / ENG-19455). API: GET /v1/optimize/suggestions Parameters: org_id (required), project_id (optional), environment (optional), route_id (optional), model (optional), suggestion_type (optional: batching|caching), risk_level (optional: low|medium|high), sort (optional), limit (default 25, max 100), cursor (opaque). Sorting: sort=estimated_savings_usd_per_day:desc (default), risk_level:asc, confidence:desc, stale_as_of:desc. Pagination: cursor-based (stable, opaque) backed by (sort_key, suggestion_id) to ensure deterministic ordering even as suggestions are recomputed. Stable IDs: suggestion_id is immutable for a given (org, project, route, model, suggestion_type) tuple; versioning is expressed via updated_at + stale_as_of rather than changing IDs. Response shape (abridged): {"data":[{"suggestion_id":"sug_01HP...","suggestion_type":"batching","scope":{"org_id":"org_...","project_id":"proj_..."},"target":{"route_id":"rt_...","route_name":"/chat/completions","model":"llama-3.1-70b-instruct"},"estimated_savings":{"usd_per_day":"42.10","percent":"18.2","token_volume_window":"24h"},"estimated_impact":{"latency_delta_ms_p50":"+12","latency_delta_ms_p95":"+38"},"risk":{"level":"medium","reasons":"Higher concurrency variance; p95 may regress during burst traffic.","guardrails":"Enable canary rollout and cap max_batch_tokens to 4096."},"confidence":{"level":"medium","inputs_missing":["cache_prefix_hit_rate"],"notes":"Derived from last 7d traffic; low samples for long prompts."},"stale_as_of":"2026-02-02T18:00:00Z","updated_at":"2026-02-02T18:05:12Z"}],"page":{"next_cursor":"eyJz..."}} Errors: consistent with existing API error envelope (code/message/request_id). Notably: 400 for invalid sort field, 403 for authn/authz (authz wired in next PR), 429 for rate limiting (defaults aligned with Console read endpoints), 5xx for upstream DB/timeouts. Implementation notes: - Adds OptimizeSuggestionsService interface + repository query with server-side ordering. - Cursor encoding uses URL-safe base64 JSON blob; validated and signed (HMAC) to prevent tampering. - Query uses composite index (org_id, project_id, sort_key, suggestion_id) to keep p95 low; falls back to updated_at ordering when sort=stale_as_of. - Includes a compatibility adapter to map internal enums to API strings (risk_level low/medium/high, suggestion_type batching/caching). Testing: - Unit tests for cursor encode/decode, invalid cursor, and deterministic paging across inserts. - API integration tests covering sort fields and filter combinations. Follow-ups: - RBAC scope optimize:suggestions:read + audit log hook (ENG-19377). - Compute pipeline job + staleness semantics (ENG-19455). - Add "assumptions" block for UI details drawer (kept out of v1 contract to reduce churn; will add behind a feature flag once copy is finalized).

title: Add tier/deployment_mode tags to SLI metrics at ingress (and propagate through request context)
preview: Context: As part of the Tiered SLO + Error Budget program, we need to compute SLIs (success rate, latency, streaming reliability) separately for Hosted (shared) vs Dedicated (reserved capacity). Today most gateway metrics are emitted without a reliable tier signal, and routing/fallback can further obscure the original customer tier in downstream services. This PR adds tier/deployment mode tags to ingress SLI metrics and ensures the tags are attached to the request context so they can be propagated into routing, traces, and downstream metrics. Summary of changes: (1) Introduce a canonical tier classifier at ingress that maps requests into tier={hosted|dedicated} and deployment_mode={hosted_api|dedicated_pool} (naming aligned with ADR-0142 draft). (2) Add these tags to the gateway SLI metric set for request count, request duration histograms, and streaming termination counters. (3) Propagate tier/deployment_mode via RequestContext (headers -> internal ctx) so routing can preserve tier across fallback/region-shift decisions and telemetry can emit consistent tags. (4) Add guardrails to avoid cardinality explosions: we do NOT add customer_id/model_id labels at ingress; those remain sampled/derived in dedicated dashboards only, behind existing access controls. (5) Provide migration notes for dashboards/recording rules: existing queries using legacy label "pool" or "account_tier" should switch to tier/deployment_mode; a compatibility recording rule is included for a 2-week deprecation window. Details: Metrics affected include api_gateway_request_total, api_gateway_request_duration_seconds_bucket, api_gateway_stream_termination_total, and api_gateway_errors_total. For streaming, we tag the termination reason classification at the gateway boundary (client_abort vs server_disconnect vs upstream_error) using the existing SSE/WebSocket close signals and a small set of heuristics; only server_disconnect and upstream_error count against streaming reliability SLI (client_abort excluded per the SLI spec). Rollout plan: This ships behind config flag telemetry.tier_tagging.enabled (default off). We’ll enable in staging for 48h, then canary 5% prod, then 100% per-region (us-east-1, us-west-2, eu-west-1) once dashboards show stable label coverage. After rollout, we will remove the compatibility recording rule and update remaining dashboards/alerts in follow-up PRs. Operational notes: If tier tagging is missing, the request is tagged tier=unknown and deployment_mode=unknown; alerts/dashboards should exclude unknown from SLO accounting (see migration note). This PR intentionally does not change SLO math; it only makes the tier dimension available and consistently propagated.
text: Context: As part of the Tiered SLO + Error Budget program, we need to compute SLIs (success rate, latency, streaming reliability) separately for Hosted (shared) vs Dedicated (reserved capacity). Today most gateway metrics are emitted without a reliable tier signal, and routing/fallback can further obscure the original customer tier in downstream services. This PR adds tier/deployment mode tags to ingress SLI metrics and ensures the tags are attached to the request context so they can be propagated into routing, traces, and downstream metrics. Summary of changes: (1) Introduce a canonical tier classifier at ingress that maps requests into tier={hosted|dedicated} and deployment_mode={hosted_api|dedicated_pool} (naming aligned with ADR-0142 draft). (2) Add these tags to the gateway SLI metric set for request count, request duration histograms, and streaming termination counters. (3) Propagate tier/deployment_mode via RequestContext (headers -> internal ctx) so routing can preserve tier across fallback/region-shift decisions and telemetry can emit consistent tags. (4) Add guardrails to avoid cardinality explosions: we do NOT add customer_id/model_id labels at ingress; those remain sampled/derived in dedicated dashboards only, behind existing access controls. (5) Provide migration notes for dashboards/recording rules: existing queries using legacy label "pool" or "account_tier" should switch to tier/deployment_mode; a compatibility recording rule is included for a 2-week deprecation window. Details: Metrics affected include api_gateway_request_total, api_gateway_request_duration_seconds_bucket, api_gateway_stream_termination_total, and api_gateway_errors_total. For streaming, we tag the termination reason classification at the gateway boundary (client_abort vs server_disconnect vs upstream_error) using the existing SSE/WebSocket close signals and a small set of heuristics; only server_disconnect and upstream_error count against streaming reliability SLI (client_abort excluded per the SLI spec). Rollout plan: This ships behind config flag telemetry.tier_tagging.enabled (default off). We’ll enable in staging for 48h, then canary 5% prod, then 100% per-region (us-east-1, us-west-2, eu-west-1) once dashboards show stable label coverage. After rollout, we will remove the compatibility recording rule and update remaining dashboards/alerts in follow-up PRs. Operational notes: If tier tagging is missing, the request is tagged tier=unknown and deployment_mode=unknown; alerts/dashboards should exclude unknown from SLO accounting (see migration note). This PR intentionally does not change SLO math; it only makes the tier dimension available and consistently propagated.

title: Audit logging: register sensitive-action event types and reject unknown event_type values
preview:
Summary: Introduces a centralized registry for audit log event_type values (sensitive actions taxonomy) and enforces that all emitted audit events use a registered event_type. Enforcement happens in two places: (1) build-time lint/codegen to prevent new event types from being introduced ad-hoc, and (2) runtime validation in the shared audit event emitter to optionally reject (or warn on) unknown types.

Motivation: As we drive to 100% coverage for sensitive actions (ENG-4822), we need consistent semantics across services and deployments (Hosted/Dedicated/Private). In the current state, event_type strings are free-form. This makes SIEM normalization brittle and makes coverage dashboards noisy (slight spelling variants create “new” types). Centralizing the taxonomy also reduces cardinality risk and improves backcompat guarantees.

Design: - Source-of-truth registry lives in repo at audit/event_types/v1/sensitive_actions.json (list of allowed event_type strings + minimal metadata used for linting and docs). - Codegen step produces language-specific constants: Go (internal/audit/types_gen.go) and TS (packages/audit-events/src/eventTypes.gen.ts). - Shared audit library validates event_type during emission: default mode is WARN in dev/test and REJECT in prod (configurable per service via AUDIT_EVENT_TYPE_ENFORCEMENT=warn|reject|off). - CI lint checks: (a) fails PR if a producer emits an event_type not in the registry, (b) fails PR if registry is modified without bumping the generated outputs, and (c) blocks non-enumerated types unless prefixed with "custom." (explicit escape hatch; still logged and surfaced as “non-sensitive/custom”).

Backcompat/rollout: This PR only adds the registry + enforcement hooks; it does not change existing producer strings except for a small handful of known typos in tests. For services that currently emit legacy/unknown values, the runtime validator can be set to WARN temporarily. Follow-up PRs will migrate remaining producers to registry values and flip enforcement to REJECT by default in prod once coverage is validated.

Test plan: - Unit tests: registry parser validation, codegen snapshot checks, and runtime validator behavior (warn vs reject). - Lint test: fixture producer intentionally emitting "rbac.role.updated" fails when registry only allows "rbac.role.update". - Integration: existing audit event schema validation tests continue to pass; this PR does not change schema.

Notes on naming: We’re aligning on <domain>.<resource>.<action> (e.g., rbac.role.update, credential.api_key.rotate, policy.routing.update). This matches the taxonomy draft discussed in #eng-security and keeps Splunk/Sentinel field values stable.

Screenshots/log output: - Example WARN (dev): "audit: unknown event_type=rbac.role.updated; allowed=...; proceeding" - Example REJECT (prod): request fails with 500 only if AUDIT_EMIT_FAILURE_MODE=block; otherwise action proceeds but audit event is dropped with explicit metric increment (see internal/audit/metrics.go).

Follow-ups: - ENG-4823/4824/4825 will update producers to use generated constants rather than raw strings. - ENG-4828 coverage dashboard will use the registry list as expected set (reduces false gaps).

Risk assessment: Low runtime risk when configured to WARN. When set to REJECT+block, misconfigured services could impact admin actions. For that reason this PR keeps default enforcement as WARN unless explicitly set by the service owner.

References: - Confluence draft: “Sensitive actions event taxonomy” and “Audit event schema v1” (workshop notes). - Slack: #eng-security thread on event_type naming + cardinality budget.

title: perf-canary: add QPS rate limits + per-region capacity budget (GPU overhead guardrail)
preview:
Context: As we move perf-canary from staging into a production region, we need hard guardrails so the canary cannot consume meaningful GPU capacity or accidentally fan out due to config mistakes. This PR implements (1) explicit QPS caps with a token-bucket limiter, and (2) a per-region capacity budget that converts an overhead target (e.g., 0.5% of region GPU capacity) into an allowed request rate/concurrency for the canary. The goal is: safe by default, predictable overhead, and an immediate kill-switch when budgets are exceeded.

What this adds (high-level): - Rate limiting: token bucket limiter at the request scheduler layer (covers both steady-state QPS and burst). - Capacity budget: `capacity_budget` config per region (defaults to 0.003 = 0.3% until we tune) with optional overrides per tier/model group. - Budget accounting: estimates GPU-seconds/request using (a) last known baseline runtime-only latency for the model+SKU, falling back to (b) a conservative static estimate when baseline is missing. - Concurrency guardrail: hard cap on in-flight requests derived from budget; limiter blocks and emits a "budget exceeded" metric instead of pushing load. - Safety valves: (a) global max_qps, (b) global max_in_flight, (c) "safe_disable" config flag to stop generating traffic while still emitting health metrics. - Telemetry: new counters/histograms so SRE can confirm overhead stays under budget and catch misconfig quickly.

Operational intent: The canary should stay <0.5% of GPU capacity per region for covered models (goal in project doc). We are starting with tighter defaults (0.3% budget, max 0.6 rps per model group, max 4 in-flight) and will loosen only after we see stable overhead in us-east rollout.

Notes/assumptions: - We treat the canary as "worst case" for overhead: no reliance on customer KV cache, no warm batching assumptions. - If baseline is stale or missing, we err toward limiting harder (pessimistic runtime estimate). - This PR does not change the prompt distribution; it just changes how quickly we run it.

Follow-ups: - Integrate budget inputs with control-plane config surface once PR-19203 lands in `redwood` (config validation + audit log). - Add per-SKU budget overrides once we have eu-west A10 vs H100 variance characterized (ENG-4882).

title: Add hot-route protection dashboards + alert rules (enterprise tier + shed reasons)
preview: Context: As part of Hot-route Capacity Protection (ENG-4926), SRE needs a consistent set of dashboards + alert rules for protected routes (/v1/chat/completions, /v1/embeddings, /v1/rerank) that make it easy to answer: (1) are enterprise routes staying within SLO under fleet pressure, (2) are we shedding/throttling the right tier first, and (3) what is the reason breakdown (quota vs overload vs admission control) when 429s occur. This PR adds Grafana dashboard definitions (jsonnet) and Prometheus alert rules for the new metrics emitted by gateway/routing/runtime (see redwood PRs for instrumentation and 429 reason codes). Scope: dashboards (per-route/per-tier latency + error + shed), annotations for protection activation events, and paging rules focused on enterprise degradation (not expected best-effort shedding). What’s included: (A) Dashboards: 1) Hot Route Protection – Overview: request volume by route group + tier, 429 rate by reason, shed/admission-control rate, queue depth / scheduler lag signals, and 5xx. 2) Hot Route Protection – Enterprise SLO: p50/p95/p99 latency by protected route group for enterprise tier, availability (2xx/3xx) and error budget burn panels, with burn windows (5m/1h/6h). 3) Hot Route Protection – Debug: top tenants (sampled/low-cardinality) by route group, saturation indicators, and correlation panels for policy-eval latency + routing decision outcomes. (B) Alerts (Prometheus rules): 1) Page: enterprise protected route latency burn > 2x for 15m OR fast burn > 10x for 5m (gated to enterprise tier + protected route groups). 2) Page: enterprise protected route 5xx rate > 1% for 10m (excluding client cancellations). 3) Ticket: sustained overload protection activation (shed_rate_overload_reason > threshold for 30m) when enterprise SLO remains healthy (expected during pressure) to drive capacity follow-up without paging storms. 4) Ticket: missing/flatlined metrics for new overload reason series (helps catch ingestion regressions and the cardinality hotfix class of issues). Notes/guardrails: All queries follow the overload metrics cardinality guidance: no tenant_id in primary panels; we use route_group, tier, region, reason_code only. The “Debug” dashboard includes an opt-in variable for sampled tenant (uses pre-aggregated topK series). Alert rules intentionally avoid paging on best-effort 429 spikes alone; we page only when enterprise SLO signals degrade. Rollout: dashboards are safe to merge before full multi-region enablement; panels will show ‘No data’ until metrics are present. Alert rules are shipped disabled by default via env toggle in the pack; enablement to follow GA checklist (eng-releases thread + canary results). Links: Linear ENG-4926 (dashboards/alerts), ENG-4927 (incident-bot notifications). Related: slo-toolkit PR for standard burn alert helper functions, redwood PR adding overload reason codes to 429s, and runtime PR exporting queue depth + scheduler lag.
