title: Add activation funnel dashboard panels + suggested alerts for regressions
preview:
Context: This is part of the Lifecycle Growth Experiments sprint (Hosted API activation + early retention). We need a standardized, self-serve dashboard that answers: (1) where do new signups drop in the funnel, (2) which experiment cohorts/variants moved the needle, and (3) how do we catch regressions quickly after email/nudge/retargeting launches.

What’s included in this PR: Adds a set of activation funnel panels to the existing Growth/PLG dashboard pack and wires in suggested (disabled-by-default) alert rules. The dashboard focuses on signup -> API key created -> first successful request -> first 100k tokens within 7d, plus TTFT distributions.

Dashboard updates: - New row: “Activation funnel (new signups)” with conversion panels per step (absolute counts + rates), and a time series of overall activation_rate_7d (first_request within 7d of signup). - Breakdown panels: conversion by region, SDK (ts/python/rest), and experiment cohort (feature flag holdout vs treatment). - Variant drilldowns: email_variant_id, nudge_id, and utm_campaign (for retargeting) as optional breakdown dimensions (these are behind dashboard variables to avoid exploding cardinality by default). - Latency: p50/p95 TTFT for first successful request (filtered to first_request events only) with a note about sampling and outlier handling. - Data quality: panel that tracks event ingestion gaps (% missing region, % missing sdk) and a warning annotation if the dedupe-fix version hasn’t fully rolled out.

Suggested alerts (added as templates, not auto-enabled): - Activation regression: activation_rate_7d drops >15% vs 28d baseline for 2h (with minimum N guardrail). - Funnel step regression: api_key_created_rate and first_request_rate drops separately, to help localize which step is broken. - TTFT regression: p95 TTFT for first request increases >25% vs baseline (suppressed if volume is low).

Notes/assumptions: - Queries expect the standardized event taxonomy from ENG-10492 (signup/api_key_created/first_request/first_100k_tokens) and will be most accurate once the duplicate-event emission fix is fully deployed (see redwood hotfix PR referenced in ENG-10492 notes). - All breakdowns default to low-cardinality dimensions; high-cardinality drilldowns are opt-in via variables.

Testing: - jsonnet render + schema validation via `make dashboards` - alert rule linting via `make alerts-lint` - manual spot-check in Grafana: panels load under 2s for last 7d; breakdowns under 5s with variables enabled.

Rollout: No runtime impact. Once merged, dashboards ship with the next observability-pack sync. Alert templates require explicit enablement per env (stage/prod) and per oncall ownership.

Screenshots: included in the PR conversation (Grafana panel previews + variable dropdowns).

title: perf-canary dashboards: add per-model p95 latency + tokens/sec panels (with breakdowns + runbook links)
preview:
## Summary
Adds the initial Grafana dashboard panels for the production performance canary. The goal is fast triage for per-model regressions (p95 latency and tokens/sec) with enough breakdowns to distinguish kernel/runtime execution regressions from scheduler/queueing and infra issues.

This PR is dashboards-only (no alert rules). Alerts are tracked separately so we can iterate on panel queries/labels before we encode anything into paging logic.

## What’s included
- New dashboard: **Perf Canary / Per-model Performance (Prod)**
  - Topline panels per selected model:
    - End-to-end latency p50/p95/p99 (histogram quantiles)
    - Runtime-only latency p50/p95/p99 (when available)
    - Tokens/sec (overall) + tokens/sec (decode-only) where emitted
  - Breakdown panels:
    - Prefill latency vs decode latency (stacked / side-by-side)
    - Queueing time (scheduler) and queueing fraction of end-to-end
    - Batch size distribution (p50/p95 + histogram heatmap)
    - KV cache hit rate (if the metric is present for the target model/runtime)
  - Correlation / attribution helpers:
    - Runtime build tag and kernel selection tag (as table + annotation)
    - GPU hardware SKU selector and region selector (to isolate fleet changes)
  - Drilldowns:
    - Explore link to raw series (preserves template vars)
    - Trace search link (where trace_id exemplars exist) for recent spikes

## Template variables (kept intentionally small)
- `model` (required)
- `region` (defaults to `us-east`, multi-select enabled)
- `tier` (hosted|dedicated|private, defaults to `hosted`)
- `hardware_sku` (optional; hidden by default to avoid casual cardinality blowups)
- `runtime_build` (optional; used primarily during rollouts)

Note: we do **not** template on `prompt_shape` / `bucket` even though canary emits shape buckets. Those panels are included as a separate collapsed row (opt-in) because we saw the label surface expand quickly during early experiments.

## Metric/query notes
- Uses the canonical perf-canary metric names agreed in ENG-4873:
  - `perf_canary_request_latency_seconds_bucket` (e2e)
  - `perf_canary_runtime_latency_seconds_bucket` (runtime-only)
  - `perf_canary_tokens_per_second` (gauge)
  - `perf_canary_queueing_seconds_bucket` (scheduler)
  - `perf_canary_batch_size` (histogram)
  - `perf_canary_kv_cache_hit_ratio` (gauge)
- Histogram quantiles are computed with `histogram_quantile()` over `rate(<bucket>[5m])`.
- Tokens/sec uses `avg_over_time(...[5m])` with a 1m min step to reduce graph jitter.
- All panels include `model`, `region`, and `tier` in the legend; other dimensions are shown only when explicitly filtered.

## Runbook links
Each top-level row includes a link to the on-call runbook for canary regressions:
- Confluence: `eng-sre/runbooks/runbook-perf-regression-canary-alert`
- The link is parameterized via dashboard variables (model/region/tier) so the runbook opens with context in the URL.

## Screenshots
- Attached in the PR UI (see: “Per-model overview row”, “Breakdown row”, “Annotations + drilldowns”).

## Rollout plan
- Land in observability-pack, then enable in `prod` Grafana folder via the normal dashboard sync job.
- Runtime/SRE will sanity check the panels during Phase 1 rollout (us-east, 2–3 models).

## Testing
- `make dashboards-lint`
- `make dashboards-render` (jsonnet render)
- Verified in staging Grafana against canary metrics (us-east) for: `llama-3.1-70b-instruct` and `qwen2.5-32b-instruct`.

## Follow-ups
- Add annotation feed for control-plane canary model list changes (so changes in coverage are obvious on the graphs).
- Add a small “alert preview” row (not rules) showing computed deviation vs baseline once baseline store is stable.

Refs: ENG-4877, ENG-4873

- `region` (defaults to `us-east`, multi-select enabled)
- `tier` (hosted|dedicated|private, defaults to `hosted`)
- `hardware_sku` (optional; hidden by default to avoid casual cardinality blowups)
- `runtime_build` (optional; used primarily during rollouts)

Note: we do **not** template on `prompt_shape` / `bucket` even though canary emits shape buckets. Those panels are included as a separate collapsed row (opt-in) because we saw the label surface expand quickly during early experiments.

## Metric/query notes
- Uses the canonical perf-canary metric names agreed in ENG-4873:
  - `perf_canary_request_latency_seconds_bucket` (e2e)
  - `perf_canary_runtime_latency_seconds_bucket` (runtime-only)
  - `perf_canary_tokens_per_second` (gauge)
  - `perf_canary_queueing_seconds_bucket` (scheduler)
  - `perf_canary_batch_size` (histogram)
  - `perf_canary_kv_cache_hit_ratio` (gauge)
- Histogram quantiles are computed with `histogram_quantile()` over `rate(<bucket>[5m])`.
- Tokens/sec uses `avg_over_time(...[5m])` with a 1m min step to reduce graph jitter.
- All panels include `model`, `region`, and `tier` in the legend; other dimensions are shown only when explicitly filtered.

## Runbook links
Each top-level row includes a link to the on-call runbook for canary regressions:
- Confluence: `eng-sre/runbooks/runbook-perf-regression-canary-alert`
- The link is parameterized via dashboard variables (model/region/tier) so the runbook opens with context in the URL.

## Screenshots
- Attached in the PR UI (see: “Per-model overview row”, “Breakdown row”, “Annotations + drilldowns”).

## Rollout plan
- Land in observability-pack, then enable in `prod` Grafana folder via the normal dashboard sync job.
- Runtime/SRE will sanity check the panels during Phase 1 rollout (us-east, 2–3 models).

## Testing
- `make dashboards-lint`
- `make dashboards-render` (jsonnet render)
- Verified in staging Grafana against canary metrics (us-east) for: `llama-3.1-70b-instruct` and `qwen2.5-32b-instruct`.

## Follow-ups
- Add annotation feed for control-plane canary model list changes (so changes in coverage are obvious on the graphs).
- Add a small “alert preview” row (not rules) showing computed deviation vs baseline once baseline store is stable.

Refs: ENG-4877, ENG-4873

title: Control plane: config surface for perf-canary target models (by region/tier) + validation + audit logging
preview: Summary: This PR adds a first-class control-plane config surface for defining which models the perf-canary should exercise, scoped by region + capacity tier, with strict validation and audit logging. This unblocks phase-1 rollout in us-east with a small model set and gives SRE/runtime a safe way to expand coverage without code changes. Motivation: We need a single source of truth for the canary target list that (a) is auditable, (b) prevents accidental wide fanout (capacity burn / metric cardinality), and (c) allows staged rollout per region and tier. Previously, target lists were hardcoded in service config and required redeploys; that’s too risky for fast tuning. Design overview: Introduces a new config kind PerfCanaryTargets stored in the control-plane config service. Adds an admin write API (RBAC-gated) for setting/updating targets and a read API scoped to the perf-canary service account. The config is keyed by (region, tier) and contains a small list of model refs (canonical model_id + optional pinned model_version), plus optional per-target overrides (enabled flag, qps_budget, max_concurrency). All writes emit an audit event containing actor, request_id, before/after hashes, and a compact diff summary. Validation / guardrails: Validation happens on write in control plane. Rules: (1) tier must be one of: hosted-shared | dedicated | private; (2) region must be a known runtime region from the region registry; (3) max 10 targets per (region,tier); (4) qps_budget must be <= 0.2 QPS per target by default unless an explicit override is provided and the requestor has the perf_canary.admin_override capability; (5) each model_id must resolve via model-registry to a canonical model slug and is normalized to the canonical id; (6) if model_version is provided, it must exist and be runnable in that region (model-registry capability check); (7) duplicate model_id+version pairs are rejected; (8) config payload size is capped and validated to avoid accidental huge lists. Audit logging: All create/update/delete operations produce an AuditEvent {event_type=PERF_CANARY_TARGETS_CONFIG_CHANGED} and include: actor_user/actor_service, request_id, region, tier, old_config_hash, new_config_hash, change_summary, and a list of model ids affected (capped to 25; remainder summarized). Audit events go to the existing audit-log sink and are also emitted in structured logs for quick grep during incidents. Auth/RBAC: New permissions: perf_canary.targets.read (granted to perf-canary SA) and perf_canary.targets.write (granted to Runtime + SRE admin groups). The read endpoint is intentionally narrow (read-only, no list-all across regions unless caller has admin). API shape (internal): GET /internal/control-plane/v1/perf-canary/targets?region=us-east&tier=hosted-shared returns the resolved config (canonical ids) plus an etag. PUT /internal/control-plane/v1/perf-canary/targets?region=...&tier=... sets full config (replace semantics). Response includes validation warnings (non-fatal) and etag. Example payload (illustrative): {"targets": [{"model_id": "llama-3.1-8b-instruct", "model_version": "2026-01-10", "enabled": true, "qps_budget": 0.05}, {"model_id": "qwen2.5-7b-instruct", "enabled": true}], "defaults": {"max_concurrency": 2}}. Notes: The perf-canary service will consume this via its existing control-plane client (separate PR in perf-canary repo wires the new endpoint). This PR focuses only on control-plane surface area, validation, and auditability. Rollout plan: Feature-flagged behind control_plane.perf_canary_targets_config (default off). Once merged, we’ll enable in staging, seed us-east/hosted-shared with 2 targets, and verify canary reads + audit trails. Follow-ups: (1) add UI in Console for editing targets (tracked separately); (2) add optional per-SKU allowlists as we expand beyond phase-1; (3) add explicit “high-risk model” tagging once the model registry exposes it.

title: Generate perf-canary baselines from controlled prod-like load runs
preview: Summary: This PR adds a repeatable baseline generator job in benchmark-lab that computes per-model performance baseline distributions from a controlled prod-like load run. The output artifacts are written in a stable, versioned format that perf-canary can consume for regression evaluation (min samples + percent-change thresholds). Motivation: perf-canary needs a durable baseline per (model, model_version, runtime_build, hardware_sku, region) that reflects real scheduler behavior (queueing/batching) without using customer traffic. Early experiments were using ad-hoc queries on ephemeral metrics, which made it hard to reproduce baselines and triggered baseline drift/cardinality issues during rollout. This job makes baselines an explicit artifact with an audit trail and a consistent schema. Scope / What’s included: (1) New job `baselinegen` that ingests a benchmark-lab load-run result bundle (request-level summary events + runtime counters) and computes: end-to-end latency histograms (p50/p95/p99 + full histogram buckets), runtime-only latency histograms, prefill and decode latency histograms, queueing time histogram, tokens/sec distribution, batch size distribution, and optional KV cache hit-rate summary when present. (2) Baseline artifact format: a single JSON artifact per dimension key, plus an index manifest for discovery. Artifacts are content-addressed and include schema versioning so perf-canary can validate compatibility. (3) Versioning & determinism: the job pins bucket boundaries, uses a seed for any sampling, and stores the exact input run id + git sha + job config in the metadata header. (4) Output location: writes to object storage under `s3://redwood-perf-baselines/perf_canary/v1/` (prod) and `s3://redwood-perf-baselines-staging/perf_canary/v1/` (staging). Includes a thin abstraction so storage backend can be swapped later without touching canary. (5) Guardrails: hard cap on dimensions written per execution (default 200) to prevent accidental wide fanout; rejects any input that contains raw prompts or request payloads (asserts only shape metrics and aggregated counters). (6) Documentation: adds `docs/perf_canary_baselines.md` describing schema, dimension keys, and how to generate/validate a baseline for a new model or hardware SKU. Non-goals: This does not change perf-canary evaluation logic or alert thresholds (handled in perf-canary repo / ADR). This does not add customer-traffic shadowing. Implementation notes: Input contract expects a `loadrun.jsonl.zst` (one line per request summary) and `runtime_metrics.pb.zst` (aggregated runtime counters) produced by existing benchmark-lab load runner. For histograms we use fixed bucket edges aligned with existing observability-pack dashboards to avoid mismatch between baseline and live metrics. The artifact JSON is intentionally flat and explicit (no nested structures beyond a small header), to keep it easy to diff/review and to allow simple validation in the canary service. Example artifact key: `model=llama-3.1-70b-instruct|model_version=2026-01-10|runtime_build=redwood-1.42.0|kernel_set=flashattn-v3|hardware_sku=h100-sxm|region=us-east-1|tier=hosted`. Testing: Unit tests cover histogram generation, percentile extraction, bucket edge stability, manifest writing, and schema validation. Integration test runs baselinegen on a small fixture loadrun bundle and asserts stable output (golden files). Added a dry-run mode that prints the computed dimension keys and sample counts without writing artifacts. Rollout plan: (1) Run job nightly in staging for the initial canary model set (2-3 models) and compare variance vs current ad-hoc baselines. (2) After sign-off from Runtime + Telemetry, enable in prod with a manual trigger path (run-id passed explicitly) before moving to scheduled runs. (3) Wire perf-canary baseline fetch to the manifest index (separate PR already in progress in perf-canary repo). Links: Linear ENG-4874 (baseline store + versioning), ENG-4872 (perf-canary MVP), ENG-4873 (metrics definitions). Notes for reviewers: Please sanity-check the dimension key policy (especially `kernel_set` and `runtime_build`), histogram bucket edges, and the cardinality guardrails. Also confirm the artifact header contains enough info for audit/debug but not enough to leak sensitive data.

title: Add region + hardware SKU dimension config (with fanout safeguards)
preview:
## Summary This PR adds first-class configuration for running perf-canary across multiple **regions** and **hardware SKUs**, while ensuring we don’t accidentally fan out to every region/SKU combination (and burn the overhead budget). The canary runner now supports: (1) Target selection by `region` and `hardware_sku` (optional), (2) A hard fanout guard that defaults to conservative behavior, and (3) Safer defaults for Helm/Env-based deployments. This is needed for Phase 1 rollout where we want to start in `us-east` on a single SKU, then expand gradually to `eu-west` and additional SKUs once baselines/thresholds are tuned.

## Motivation / Context Today the canary targets are effectively “global” (implicitly whatever the control plane routes to). That makes it hard to: - Attribute regressions to fleet changes (SKU-specific) vs runtime changes - Set region-specific baselines (telemetry variance differs per region) - Control cost/overhead during rollout (avoid accidental wide fanout)

This change is intentionally scoped to **config + selection + safety rails**. Baselines/threshold wiring is handled by baseline fetch logic in a separate PR.

## Design - **Target dimensions:** `region`, `hardware_sku` are added as *optional* dimensions to the canary target config. If `hardware_sku` is omitted, the canary runs “per region” and relies on the inference tier routing for SKU; if provided, we force requests to that SKU via the internal routing hint header (same mechanism used by benchmark-lab).
- **Fanout safeguards:** - New config key: `fanout_max_targets` (default: `3`) - New config key: `fanout_allowlist` (optional explicit list of `<region>:<sku>` tuples). If set, only those tuples run. - New startup-time safety check: if computed target count exceeds `fanout_max_targets`, the process exits non-zero unless `--allow-fanout-over-max` is explicitly set (intended only for controlled manual runs). - New “blast radius” log line on startup prints the exact computed targets and the derived capacity budget per target.
- **Metric labels / cardinality:** Region is now a standard label on canary-emitted metrics. Hardware SKU is only emitted when explicitly configured (to avoid accidental cardinality growth); when omitted, we emit `hardware_sku="unknown"` for aggregator stability.
- **Alert payload:** Alert webhook payload includes `region` + `hardware_sku` in the `scope` section so incident-bot threads are dedupe-able per tuple.

## Config changes - New config schema fields (YAML): - `targets.regions` (list) - `targets.hardware_skus` (list, optional) - `targets.matrix_mode` (one of: `region_only`, `region_x_sku`) - `safety.fanout_max_targets` (int) - `safety.fanout_allowlist` (list of strings `region:sku` or `region:*`) - `safety.require_explicit_region` (bool, default true)

Example (Phase 1): ```yaml targets:   regions: ["us-east-1"]   matrix_mode: "region_only" safety:   fanout_max_targets: 2   require_explicit_region: true ```

Example (Phase 2 w/ SKU): ```yaml targets:   regions: ["us-east-1", "eu-west-1"]   hardware_skus: ["h100-sxm", "a100-80gb"]   matrix_mode: "region_x_sku" safety:   fanout_allowlist: ["us-east-1:h100-sxm", "eu-west-1:h100-sxm"]   fanout_max_targets: 3 ```

## Implementation notes - Added `TargetMatrix` helper to compute the final run list. This keeps selection logic out of the request loop and makes it unit-testable. - Added request routing hints: `X-Redwood-Region` and `X-Redwood-Hardware-Sku` (only when configured). These are already supported server-side for internal callers; the perf-canary service account is restricted to internal endpoints. - Extended the runner loop to include `target_scope` in logs and metric tags.

## Tests - Unit tests for matrix expansion, allowlist filtering, max-target guard, and stable ordering (so rollouts are deterministic). - Updated config loader tests to validate schema errors (e.g., `region_x_sku` with missing `hardware_skus`). - Added a small “dry-run” mode test that prints computed targets and exits.

## Rollout plan - Merge behind conservative defaults: `require_explicit_region=true`, `fanout_max_targets=3`, `matrix_mode=region_only`. - Phase 1 (ENG-4880): enable `us-east-1` only, no SKU dimension. - Phase 2: introduce SKU dimension for `h100-sxm` only, validate baseline alignment, then expand.

## Risks / Mitigations - Risk: accidental fanout increases load/cost. Mitigation: hard guard + allowlist option + explicit override flag. - Risk: metrics cardinality blowup. Mitigation: hardware_sku label only when explicitly configured; region label is bounded and already present in most dashboards.

## Screenshots / Output (dry-run) - `--dry-run` output example: `computed_targets=[us-east-1:*] target_count=1 fanout_max_targets=3` - `region_x_sku` example: `computed_targets=[us-east-1:h100-sxm, eu-west-1:h100-sxm] target_count=2 fanout_allowlist=2`

## Follow-ups - Baseline store needs to key off (model, runtime_version, sku, region) once we enable SKU dimension broadly. - Confirm incident-bot dedupe keys include region+sku (separate PR in incident-bot repo).

title: Add log redaction + no-PII assertions for perf-canary request/telemetry path
preview: Motivation: perf-canary must never emit or persist customer data. While the canary uses synthetic prompts, we still had a few sharp edges where (a) debug logging could print request bodies on errors and (b) some error/reporting paths would include raw prompt text / user-provided identifiers if they ever slipped through config. This PR adds explicit redaction + hard assertions so we fail-closed and keep logs/metrics safe. Summary of changes: (1) Introduce a centralized redaction helper for log fields and error payloads; (2) Add runtime assertions that enforce "synthetic-only" prompt sources (no external prompt injection), with an opt-out only for local dev; (3) Ensure we never log request bodies, prompt strings, or headers that may contain identifiers; (4) Add "safe fields" allowlist for alert webhook payloads so incident-bot never receives prompt content; (5) Add tests that simulate error paths (HTTP 4xx/5xx, timeouts, JSON decode errors) and verify log output + alert payload are redacted; (6) Add lightweight "no_pii" static checks on structured logging calls in canary request loop. Operational notes: if assertions trip in prod, the canary request will be dropped and counted under canary_safety_dropped_requests_total with reason=no_pii_guard; the process stays healthy and continues other model targets. title: Add internal perf-canary auth scope + restricted service account for inference reads
preview:
Context: The perf-canary service (see perf-canary repo PRs) needs to call internal inference endpoints continuously from prod. We do not want to reuse broad internal scopes (or customer-style API keys) because (a) blast radius is too large, (b) auditing is noisy, and (c) it complicates incident response when we need to quickly disable only canary traffic. This PR introduces a restricted auth scope + service account intended solely for synthetic canary inference reads.

What’s in this PR:
- New auth scope: `inference.canary:read` (internal-only). This scope can only be minted for service accounts (not user tokens) and is denied in external auth flows.
- New service account type + default policy bundle: `svc-perf-canary` (control-plane managed). The policy allows:
  - POST `/v1/chat/completions`, `/v1/completions` against internal inference gateways
  - streaming responses
  - read-only access to model metadata needed for request normalization (canonical model + version)
  And explicitly denies:
  - any admin/control-plane routes
  - any write/update routes (model registry writes, routing config, quotas)
  - any endpoint that could return customer prompts/logs
- Gateway enforcement: inference gateway now recognizes `inference.canary:read` and applies additional constraints:
  - requires `X-Redwood-Canary: true` header (prevents accidental reuse)
  - requires `X-Redwood-Caller: perf-canary` (auditing/dedupe key)
  - enforces per-request caps: `max_tokens <= 512`, `n == 1`, `logprobs == false`, `store == false`
  - disables tool/function calling for canary scope (reduces surface area + variance)
- Audit logging: adds structured auth/audit fields `caller_type=service_account`, `service_account=svc-perf-canary`, `auth_scope=inference.canary:read` on inference gateway logs so we can correlate canary-driven load and quickly validate that the canary is not calling broader routes.
- Documentation: updates internal auth scope registry docs with ownership + rotation expectations for this scope.

Why this design:
- Least privilege: canary needs “generate tokens” but should not be able to mutate config or read any customer data.
- Fast kill-switch: we can revoke the service account or deny-list the scope at the gateway without impacting other internal tooling.
- Predictable observability: dedicated scope + headers make it easy to filter canary traffic from production dashboards and to attach clear attribution during incidents.

Operational notes:
- This PR does not create the service account secret/IAM bindings; that is wired in terraform (tracked under INT-2213 and ENG-4879). This PR only adds the scope, gateway enforcement, and policy plumbing.
- Backwards compatibility: existing internal inference callers are unchanged. The new scope is additive.

Test plan:
- Unit: added auth policy evaluation tests ensuring `svc-perf-canary` cannot access denied routes and cannot exceed request caps.
- Integration (staging): exercised canary token against internal gateway with synthetic requests; verified 200s on allowed routes and 403s/422s on denied/invalid requests.
- Observability: verified audit log fields present and that `X-Redwood-Canary` traffic is filterable in log explorer.

Rollout:
1) Merge this PR.
2) Apply terraform to create `svc-perf-canary` and mint a token restricted to `inference.canary:read`.
3) Deploy perf-canary with new token + required headers.
4) Monitor `inference_gateway_authz_denied_total{scope="inference.canary:read"}` and request volume for 24h.

Notes for reviewers:
- Policy lives under `authz/policies/service_accounts/perf_canary.yaml`.
- Gateway constraints are intentionally conservative to reduce both variance and abuse potential. If we need tool calling later for coverage, we should add a separate scope rather than expanding this one.

Related:
- perf-canary service skeleton: sources/github/perf-canary/pr-19201-bootstrap-perf-canary-service-skeleton.json
- Control plane config surface for canary targets: PR-19203
- Service account + secrets wiring (terraform): tracked under INT-2213 / ENG-4879

title: perf-canary/telemetry: add per-model metrics aggregation + normalization helper
preview: Motivation: perf-canary and the Grafana dashboard queries need a consistent definition of per-model metrics across services (canary runner, runtime telemetry pipeline, and alert evaluation). We currently have multiple slightly-different implementations of: unit conversions (ms vs s), histogram bucket alignment, label normalization (model vs model_id vs model_version), and derived signals (tokens/sec from token_count + duration). These differences are causing alert/query mismatches and make regression triage slower. This PR introduces a shared helper for per-model aggregation + normalization, intended to be used by perf-canary (PR-19218) and by the metrics pipeline/queries feeding dashboards/alerts. Summary of changes: (1) Add PerModelNormalizer + helpers to produce canonical metric names, units, and histogram buckets; (2) Add derived metric builders (e2e_latency_ms, runtime_latency_ms, tokens_per_sec) with consistent math and guardrails; (3) Introduce a small label sanitizer to enforce the agreed label set (model, model_version, region, hardware_sku, runtime_build, kernel_id) and drop high-cardinality/unapproved labels; (4) Provide a compatibility shim for existing metric names so dashboards don’t break during rollout; (5) Add unit tests with golden bucket boundaries + sample inputs from perf-canary staging runs. Behavior details: - Latency units: canonical is milliseconds (ms). Any inputs in seconds are converted to ms at ingest. - Histograms: canonical buckets (ms) are [5, 10, 25, 50, 75, 100, 150, 200, 300, 500, 750, 1000, 1500, 2000, 3000, 5000, 10000] with a separate tighter set for runtime-only latency. The helper exports both and chooses based on metric kind. - tokens/sec: computed as (output_tokens / decode_wall_time_seconds). If decode_wall_time is missing, fallback to (output_tokens / total_wall_time_seconds) but emits a counter indicating fallback path for visibility. - Model identifiers: canonicalizes to model=<registry_name> and model_version=<semver-ish or digest> where available; if only a legacy model_id exists, maps through the registry resolver interface (pluggable) and emits a warning-level log once per process (rate-limited) when mapping fails. - Label policy: drops request_id, tenant_id, route, and prompt_shape from emitted metrics by default (they can still exist in logs/traces), to avoid cardinality blowups. Testing: - Unit tests cover unit conversion, histogram bucket selection, derived tokens/sec math, and label filtering. - Added a small fuzz-ish test around label sanitizer to ensure we don’t accidentally permit new labels without an allowlist update. Rollout plan: - Land helper behind an internal package path and adopt it in perf-canary next. - Keep compatibility names for 2 weeks while dashboards migrate; after that we can remove the old names and update the alerts in observability-pack. Checklist: [x] Backward compatible metric names for existing dashboards; [x] Cardinality review: only allowlisted labels; [x] Added tests and golden buckets; [x] Added docs in code comments for canonical metric contract. Notes: This does not change runtime emission yet; it only standardizes aggregation/normalization on the consumer side. A follow-up will migrate the runtime exporter to use the same bucket boundaries for end-to-end consistency.

title: Add synthetic prompt generator + prompt-shape buckets (seedable workload profiles)
preview: Summary: This PR adds a synthetic prompt-shape generator and a seedable workload profile so perf-canary runs are reproducible across regions/hosts and can be tuned without touching code. The goal is to approximate production request shapes (prefill/decode mix, prompt length buckets) without using any customer prompts or identifiers. Why: Baselines and regression detection require stable distributions. Early canary scaffolding used a fixed prompt and random lengths, which made p95 latency and tokens/sec variance too high and made comparisons across runtime versions noisy. What’s included: 1) PromptShape + WorkloadProfile config types (YAML) with explicit length buckets and weights; 2) Seeded RNG for selecting shapes, generation params, and per-run determinism; 3) A small library that produces “prompt skeletons” (shape only) that can be filled by a static, non-sensitive text corpus shipped with the service; 4) Guardrails to prevent accidental generation of unique/high-cardinality prompts (bounded vocabulary + truncation + stable templates). Non-goals: This does not attempt to match semantic content, tool-calling schemas, or customer-specific formatting. This is intentionally shape-only for privacy and stability. Follow-ups: Add a control-plane surface for selecting profiles per model/region, and tune weights based on aggregated production histograms (without collecting prompts).

title: Emit metrics for end-to-end latency and tokens/sec (per-model) with cardinality guardrails
preview: Summary: This PR adds first-pass metric emission for perf-canary runs: end-to-end latency histograms and tokens/sec (plus supporting counters) with per-model attribution. Metrics are emitted from the canary process (not the runtime) so we can alert even when runtime telemetry pipelines are delayed. Motivation: We need low-noise, per-model signals for p50/p95/p99 latency and throughput (tokens/sec) before we can wire dashboards/alerting. This also sets the label policy up front to avoid a repeat of past cardinality blowups (e.g., per-prompt identifiers or unconstrained model strings). Metrics added (Prometheus/OpenTelemetry bridge): perf_canary_request_e2e_latency_ms (histogram; attributes: model_id, model_version, region, tier, hardware_sku, outcome); perf_canary_request_tokens_per_second (histogram; computed from generated_tokens / decode_time; same attributes); perf_canary_generated_tokens_total (counter; same attributes); perf_canary_requests_total (counter; same attributes); perf_canary_metric_dropped_total (counter; attributes: metric_name, drop_reason) to make guardrail drops visible. Cardinality guardrails: model_id + model_version are sourced from control-plane config and validated against an allowlist at startup; we reject/replace unknown model identifiers with model_id="unknown" and increment perf_canary_metric_dropped_total (drop_reason=unknown_model). We also hard-cap unique label sets per process (default 500) to fail closed (drop + log) if someone accidentally adds a high-card dimension. Explicitly not emitted: prompt text, prompt hashes, request ids, customer ids, raw error strings. outcome is normalized to a small enum (ok | timeout | http_error | inference_error | cancelled). Notes: tokens/sec is computed as generated_tokens / max(decode_wall_ms, 1ms) and emitted only for successful runs; for failures we still emit latency + counters to avoid survivorship bias in alerting. Follow-ups: This PR does not include baseline comparison logic (see PR-19218) or region/SKU fanout config (PR-19226).

title: Runtime: emit build + kernel selection/version tags in telemetry
preview: Context: perf-canary + SRE triage needs to quickly answer “what changed?” when p95 latency or tokens/sec regresses. Today we can see model/version + hardware, but we can’t reliably correlate to runtime build or kernel selection changes (especially when selection is dynamic). This PR adds low-cardinality tags to runtime telemetry (metrics + traces + request debug logs) to identify the runtime build and the kernel selection outcome for a request. These tags are explicitly designed for per-model aggregation and alert attribution and should be stable across rollouts. What’s included: (1) Runtime build tags: runtime.build_id (semver-ish build label from build pipeline), runtime.git_sha (short SHA), runtime.build_flavor (release|debug), runtime.feature_set (compile-time feature bitset exported as a short string; see below). (2) Kernel selection identifiers: kernel.bundle_version (version string for the kernel pack baked into the runtime image), kernel.selector_version (version for selection logic), kernel.policy (e.g. auto|pinned|compat), kernel.id (stable identifier for the chosen kernel impl), kernel.variant (e.g. fp16|bf16|int8 where applicable). (3) Guardrails to keep cardinality bounded: kernel.id is a curated stable name (NOT a per-build hash), kernel.variant is a small enum, runtime.feature_set is a fixed-format string with a bounded keyspace. We intentionally do NOT tag with full PTX/cubin hashes or per-host identifiers. (4) Backward compatibility: all tags are optional; exporters will omit on older runtimes. Dashboards/queries can use label_replace/defaults safely. (5) Tests: unit tests for tag formatting + allowed-value validation, and a golden telemetry test that asserts emitted label set for a representative request. Notes/decisions: - runtime.build_id source of truth is the build pipeline env var RUNTIME_BUILD_ID (already present in release builds but not previously emitted). We fall back to “unknown” in dev builds to avoid breaking local runs. - kernel.bundle_version is sourced from kernels/manifest.json embedded at build time; selector_version is incremented with changes to heuristics (documented in code). - For tracing, we add these as span attributes on the “runtime.decode” and “runtime.prefill” spans (not per-token spans) to avoid overhead. - For metrics, tags are applied only to the canary/perf aggregation metrics and a small set of existing runtime histograms that are already sampled/aggregated; we did not add tags to high-volume per-request counters. Rollout: safe to ship behind TELEMETRY_EMIT_RUNTIME_KERNEL_TAGS=true (default on in prod builds after this merges). We can flip it off quickly if there’s any telemetry cost surprise. Follow-ups: update perf-canary dashboard links and alert payload formatter to include these fields (separate PR in incident-bot/observability-pack).

title: Add audit event schema library + JSON Schema validator (v1 draft)
preview: Summary: This PR introduces a shared audit event library in the monorepo to standardize emission of security audit logs across services. It adds (1) strongly-typed structs for AuditEvent v1 (draft) with consistent field naming, (2) a JSON Schema artifact + validator that runs at runtime (and in tests) to catch malformed events early, and (3) example producers + unit tests to demonstrate correct usage in the control-plane and API gateway. Motivation: We’ve had drift across services (missing actor fields, inconsistent correlation IDs, ad-hoc target naming), which makes downstream normalization/export and customer SIEM mapping brittle. This is the foundation for ENG-4822+ (event_type registry + lints) and the service-by-service instrumentation rollouts. Scope: Adds new package `pkg/auditlog` (types, helpers, schema); adds `schemas/audit/audit-event-v1.schema.json`; adds test utilities and golden fixtures; wires minimal examples in two services behind a feature flag. Non-goals: This does not attempt to fully enumerate sensitive actions or retrofit existing producers (tracked in follow-up PRs). Implementation details: - `pkg/auditlog/event.go`: `AuditEventV1` struct with required fields; `NewEvent()` helper enforces non-empty `event_id`, `event_type`, `occurred_at`, `tenant_id` and populates common context from request-scoped metadata where available. - `pkg/auditlog/validate.go`: `ValidateJSON([]byte) error` and `ValidateEvent(AuditEventV1) error` using embedded JSON Schema (go:embed) + compiled schema cache. title: Bootstrap perf-canary service skeleton (config + request loop)
preview:
Summary: This PR bootstraps the initial perf-canary service skeleton: repo layout, config parsing/validation, and a basic request loop that can hit internal inference endpoints on a schedule. This is intentionally minimal (no metrics emission yet, no baseline evaluation yet) so we can land the scaffolding early and iterate behind feature flags.

Context: We need a lightweight, always-on canary workload in prod to catch serving-runtime regressions (p95 latency + tokens/sec) before broad customer impact. This PR is the first building block: a small service that can execute synthetic requests against a curated model list and report per-run status via logs. Follow-ups will add: (1) synthetic prompt generation (shape-only; no customer prompts), (2) structured metrics + histograms, (3) baseline fetch + regression evaluation, and (4) rate limiting/capacity budgeting.

What’s in this PR:
- New Go service under services/perf-canary/ with a single binary (cmd/perf-canary).
- Config layout and validation:
  - YAML config file + env overrides (PERF_CANARY_*).
  - Target definitions: region, endpoint, model, request type.
  - Schedule: interval + jitter.
  - Safety defaults: low QPS (1 worker), strict timeouts, and a hard max on concurrent in-flight requests.
- Basic request loop:
  - Iterates over configured targets and issues a request using an internal service token (provided via env; token wiring done elsewhere).
  - Captures timing spans (wall clock end-to-end) and logs: target, model, status code, elapsed_ms.
  - Does NOT log request/response bodies.
- Minimal client implementation:
  - HTTP client with per-request timeout.
  - Retry policy: 1 retry on 5xx / transient network errors (with bounded backoff).
- Unit tests for config parsing and validation; a small “smoke” integration test that runs against a local stub server.
- README with local dev instructions and example config.

Non-goals (explicitly not in this PR):
- No metrics emission (Prometheus/OTel) yet.
- No baseline storage/comparison.
- No prompt generator (requests use a placeholder short synthetic prompt string; follow-up PR will replace with shape generator + seed control).
- No k8s/Helm/Terraform deployment artifacts.

Operational/Safety notes:
- The service currently logs only metadata (model/target + timings). No prompt text, user identifiers, or response content.
- Config includes a “dry_run” mode that only validates config and prints the planned schedule/targets.

Testing:
- go test ./... (unit + stubbed integration)
- gofmt/goimports

Rollout plan:
- Merge behind a disabled-by-default config. We’ll wire deployment + service account in subsequent PRs and start in a single region with 2–3 models.

Related:
- ENG-4872 (Perf canary prod MVP)
- ENG-4873 (Define per-model regression metrics; upcoming)

Screenshots/log examples (from stub run):
- INFO run completed target=us-east-internal model=llama-3.1-8b status=200 elapsed_ms=312
- WARN request failed target=us-east-internal model=llama-3.1-8b status=503 elapsed_ms=1001 retry=1

title: runtime: export scheduler queueing-time metrics + batch-size distribution
preview: Motivation: perf-canary is showing end-to-end latency regressions that are hard to attribute (kernel/runtime execution vs scheduler pressure). This PR exports scheduler queueing-time metrics and batch-size distribution at the runtime layer so we can: (1) split latency into queueing vs execution for canary attribution, (2) detect regressions caused by batching heuristics / admission control changes, (3) avoid relying on sampling-heavy trace-only approaches. What’s included: - New histogram metric for scheduler queueing time (enqueue -> execution start). - New histogram metric for batch size at execution start (effective batch after coalescing). - Structured dimension set aligned with telemetry cardinality constraints (no per-request identifiers). - Wiring to existing runtime metrics registry + export path. - Unit tests for metric emission + bounds (no negative durations, no zero/negative batch sizes). Metric definitions (names / units): - redwood_runtime_scheduler_queueing_time_ms (histogram, ms) - redwood_runtime_scheduler_batch_size (histogram, count) - redwood_runtime_scheduler_queueing_time_ms_count / _sum / _bucket (exported by histogram backend) Dimensions (labels): model (canonical name), model_version (registry version id), region, tier (hosted|dedicated|private), pool (logical serving pool), hardware_sku, runtime_build_id, scheduler_policy (coarse enum). Notes: - scheduler_policy is intentionally coarse ("classic" | "continuous" | "unknown") to avoid config-cardinality blowups. - We do NOT emit request_id, tenant_id, prompt_length, or any user identifiers. - Metrics are emitted at the point a request leaves the scheduler queue and is assigned to a GPU execution slot; for batched execution, queueing time is tracked per request, and batch_size is tracked per batch. - Backfill: none; this is new time series. Canary and dashboards can start querying once the runtime rolls out. Rollout plan: ship behind runtime flag RuntimeMetrics.SchedulerQueueing (default ON in staging, ON in prod after canary validates). If metrics volume is higher than expected, we can disable via the flag without runtime restart (dynamic config refresh). Validation: verified under synthetic load in staging (benchmark-lab runner) that queueing time rises with induced scheduler saturation and returns to baseline when load is removed; batch-size histogram shifts as expected when toggling continuous batching depth. Related: ENG-4875 adds scheduler-level instrumentation; perf-canary dashboards/alerts will follow in observability-pack. Screenshots: none (metrics-only).

title: Add schema + SIEM mappings for offline bundle verification audit events
preview:
Context: Redwood Private offline bundle signing/verifying is landing for air-gapped installs. The installer now emits local audit events for bundle verification outcomes (success + specific failure reasons). Customers who forward audit logs to a SIEM via audit-log-exporter need these events to be recognized, normalized, and exported in a stable schema.

What this PR does:
- Adds new event definitions to audit-log-exporter’s schema catalog for bundle verification outcomes.
- Adds normalization/mapping rules for JSON (native), CEF, and LEEF output modes.
- Ensures failure reasons are exported as a bounded enum + optional details field (redaction-aware).
- Updates unit tests + golden fixtures for all output formats.

New events (normalized):
- redwood.private.bundle_verification.succeeded
- redwood.private.bundle_verification.failed

Normalized fields:
- event.name (string)
- event.outcome (success|failure)
- event.time (RFC3339)
- redwood.bundle.id (string, build id / bundle build identifier)
- redwood.bundle.version (string, semantic version)
- redwood.bundle.manifest_sha256 (string)
- redwood.bundle.signature.key_id (string)
- redwood.bundle.signature.trust (trusted|untrusted|unknown)
- redwood.bundle.verify.failure_reason (enum; only when failure)
- redwood.bundle.verify.failure_detail (string; optional; redacted by default)
- host.name / cluster.id (when available in exporter input)

Failure reason enum (initial set):
- MANIFEST_HASH_MISMATCH
- ARTIFACT_HASH_MISMATCH
- SIGNATURE_INVALID
- SIGNING_KEY_UNTRUSTED
- SIGNING_KEY_UNKNOWN
- SIGNATURE_EXPIRED
- SIGNATURE_NOT_YET_VALID
- DOWNGRADE_BLOCKED
- BUNDLE_LAYOUT_INVALID
- VERIFICATION_SKIPPED_BREAK_GLASS

Notes:
- We intentionally avoid exporting file paths / artifact names by default (can leak customer topology); failure_detail is scrubbed unless operator sets `redaction.allow_sensitive=true`.
- CEF/LEEF mappings keep the set small (key_id, bundle_id, version, outcome, failure_reason). We add a single extension field for manifest_sha256.

Why now:
- Pilot customers are already asking why their SIEM doesn’t show verification events while local logs clearly do (see SUP-31910 for a post-rotation unknown key case).

Docs:
- Internal: aligns with the audit event schema review thread and the Private on-prem audit event definition.

Rollout:
- Backward compatible. If events are not present, exporter behavior unchanged. If present, they are recognized and included in the output stream.

Testing:
- Unit tests for schema registration
- Golden output tests for JSON/CEF/LEEF
- Fuzz test for failure_reason parsing (ensures unknown values map to OTHER and do not crash the pipeline)

Follow-ups (not in this PR):
- Add dashboards/queries snippets for Splunk + Sentinel (tracked in ENG-4850 enablement work)
- Add per-tenant routing rules for customers who want bundle verification events in a separate index

Screenshots/Examples:
- JSON output example and CEF mapping included in test fixtures + PR notes below.

Risk:
- Low runtime risk; schema-only change. Main risk is field naming drift; mitigated by golden fixtures and explicit enum list.

title: Post overload-protection activations to #incidents with dashboards + runbook links
preview:
## Summary
This PR adds a new incident-bot event handler for Hot-route Capacity Protection activation events emitted by routing/gateway. When protection enters an active state (or exits), incident-bot posts a structured Slack message to `#incidents` with:
- route_group + route (when available), region, tier class, and activation type
- primary reason code(s) (quota vs overload) and protection mode (admission control vs shedding)
- current/peak metrics snapshot (shed_rate, 429_rate, p95 latency, queue depth where present)
- links to the SRE dashboard + runbook section
- a dedupe key to avoid paging storms during flappy thresholds

This is intended to reduce time-to-diagnosis during overload events and make it obvious when best-effort is being throttled to preserve enterprise SLOs.

## Context
Hot-route capacity protection is rolling out behind flags (see ENG-4927). During the us-east canary we observed multiple on-call threads asking “are we currently shedding?” and “which runbook section applies?”. A lightweight, deterministic bot post was the fastest way to improve shared situational awareness.

## Behavior
### When we post
- On transition into ACTIVE (per region + route_group + tier_class)
- On transition out of ACTIVE (recovery)
- Optional periodic reminder every 20 minutes while ACTIVE if shed_rate remains above threshold (disabled by default; behind `INCIDENT_BOT_OVERLOAD_REMINDER_ENABLED`)

### Dedupe / rate limiting
- Dedupe key: `(region, route_group, tier_class, mode)`
- Minimum repeat interval: 10 minutes (configurable)
- If the source emits frequent toggles, we collapse and post an update message (thread reply) instead of new top-level posts.

### Message format (example)
Top-level post:
- Title: `Overload protection ACTIVE: us-east / chat_completions / enterprise` 
- Fields:
  - Mode: `admission_control + shedding`
  - Reason: `OVERLOAD_QUEUE_DEPTH` (plus any secondary reason codes)
  - Shed rate (best-effort): `12.4% (5m)`
  - 429 rate (enterprise): `0.3% (5m)`
  - p95 latency (enterprise): `820ms (5m)`
- Links:
  - Dashboard: `Hot Route Protection / us-east / chat_completions`
  - Runbook: `Runbook: Overload protection and admission control (Diagnose)`
  - Debug endpoint (internal): `Admission decision inspector` (only shown if `INCIDENT_BOT_SHOW_DEBUG_LINK=true`)

Recovery post:
- Title: `Overload protection RECOVERED: us-east / chat_completions / enterprise`
- Includes duration in active state and a small “before/after” snapshot.

## Implementation notes
- New event type: `overload_protection.activation.v1`
- Source payload is treated as untrusted input; all fields are schema-validated and truncated to keep Slack payload small.
- We intentionally avoid embedding high-cardinality identifiers (tenant_id, request_id) in the Slack message. The dashboard link includes filters but not tenant scoping.
- Confluence + dashboard links are generated from a simple mapping keyed by `route_group` and `region`.

## Config
- `INCIDENT_BOT_OVERLOAD_POSTS_ENABLED` (default: false)
- `INCIDENT_BOT_OVERLOAD_CHANNEL` (default: #incidents)
- `INCIDENT_BOT_OVERLOAD_DEDUPE_MINUTES` (default: 10)
- `INCIDENT_BOT_OVERLOAD_REMINDER_ENABLED` (default: false)
- `INCIDENT_BOT_SHOW_DEBUG_LINK` (default: false)

## Testing
- Unit tests for:
  - schema validation + truncation
  - dedupe + threading behavior
  - link generation
  - ACTIVE -> ACTIVE updates vs ACTIVE -> RECOVERED transitions
- Manual test against Slack sandbox workspace using the replay CLI.

## Rollout plan
1) Merge behind flag (this PR)
2) Enable only in staging for 48h; validate dedupe + message formatting
3) Canary enable in us-east for protected routes only; verify no spam during expected best-effort shedding
4) Expand to other regions post GA enablement window

## Links
- Linear: ENG-4927
- Runbook: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ENGSRE/Runbook%3A+Overload+protection+and+admission+control
- Dashboard spec: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ENGPLAT/Dashboard+spec%3A+Hot-route+protection
- Related PRs:
  - redwood/pr-27441-add-overload-reason-codes-to-429-responses
  - observability-pack/pr-1832-add-hot-route-protection-dashboards

Checklist:
- [x] Includes feature flag default-off
- [x] Includes dedupe protections
- [x] Includes tests
- [x] No high-cardinality fields in message text
- [x] Docs link points to correct runbook section

title: Add perf-canary alert handler (Slack threads, dedupe, runbook links)
preview:
Summary: Adds a first-class handler in incident-bot for perf-canary regression alerts. The handler formats alerts into a consistent Slack thread, de-dupes repeated firings, and attaches deep links (Grafana dashboard + Confluence runbook + relevant traces) to speed triage.

Context: The perf-canary service will emit alerts as a webhook payload (model + region + tier + metric + observed vs baseline + confidence + baseline version/runtime tags). Before this PR, incident-bot treated canary payloads as generic webhooks which led to noisy, non-threaded posts and no reliable dedupe key, making it hard to triage during regressions and especially during baseline drift/threshold tuning.

What this PR does: 1) Adds a new webhook route + handler for source=perf-canary (Content-Type: application/json). 2) Builds a stable dedupe key and thread key so repeat evaluations (same model/metric/region/severity) update the same Slack thread instead of spamming the channel. 3) Produces a compact Slack message with: top-line regression summary; baseline vs observed; min-samples/confidence; tags (model_version/runtime_build/kernel_tag/hw_sku); and quick links. 4) Adds a lightweight state store interface for dedupe/threads (Redis-backed in prod; in-memory fake for tests). 5) Adds guardrails: drop oversized payload fields, strip unknown labels, and redact any prompt-like fields (defense-in-depth even though canary is synthetic).

Dedupe/threading behavior: - Dedupe key: perf_canary|<env>|<model_id>|<model_version>|<region>|<tier>|<metric>|<severity>|<baseline_id>|<runtime_build>|<hw_sku>. - Thread key: perf_canary|<env>|<model_id>|<region>|<tier>|<metric>|<severity>. - If a new alert matches an existing thread key within the configured TTL (default 6h), we post a reply in-thread and update the thread header (Slack message update) with the most recent observed values. - If severity escalates (warning -> page), we start/attach to the paging thread and include an explicit "Escalated" note with timestamps.

Links: - Runbook: sources/confluence/eng-sre/runbooks/runbook-perf-regression-canary-alert (hardcoded base + slug; environment-aware). - Grafana: deep link to per-model dashboard with prefilled variables (model, region, tier, hw_sku) and time range anchored around first_seen. - Traces/logs: link templates for Tempo/Loki queries using request_route=perf-canary and model_id.

Testing: - Unit tests cover dedupe key generation, Slack block rendering, and TTL behavior. - Integration test covers "first alert creates thread" + "repeat alert updates thread" + "escalation".

Rollout: - Disabled by default behind flag incident_bot.perf_canary.enabled (default false). - Staging enabled for #eng-runtime-canary; prod enablement tracked in ENG-4878.

Checklist: - [x] Adds handler behind feature flag - [x] Slack formatting aligned with SRE paging hygiene - [x] Dedupe TTL configurable - [x] Runbook + dashboard links included - [x] Tests added - [x] Backward compatible with existing generic webhook handler

title: Policy engine: add priority classes and per-route budgets (deterministic decision logging + validation)
preview: Summary: This PR extends the policy engine to support (a) explicit priority classes (enterprise/standard/best-effort/internal) and (b) per-route budgets that can be consumed deterministically during admission control / overload protection. It also adds deterministic decision logging (stable reason codes + structured fields) and a schema validation layer to keep policies auditable for enterprise escalations. Motivation: Hot-route capacity protection requires a single, explainable place to answer: "given tenant, route group, and current capacity signals, should we admit / throttle / shed / fallback". We need to do this with minimal per-request overhead and without introducing high-cardinality telemetry. This PR focuses on correctness + determinism; performance tuning (memoization/batched lookups) will follow. What’s in scope: 1) Policy schema additions: - priority_class: enum {INTERNAL, ENTERPRISE, STANDARD, BEST_EFFORT} (default STANDARD for backward compatibility) - route_group: canonical name for grouping endpoints (e.g., chat_completions, embeddings, rerank) - budgets: optional per route_group budgets keyed by priority class (requests/sec + burst + optional concurrency cap) - actions: allow | throttle | shed | fallback (fallback gated; final enforcement happens in gateway/routing) 2) Policy evaluation contract: - input includes: tenant_id, tier, route, route_group, region, and coarse capacity signals (queue_depth_bucket, gpu_saturation_bucket, scheduler_lag_bucket) - output includes: decision, decision_reason, retry_after_ms (optional), chosen_priority_class, matched_rule_id, and a stable decision_hash for auditability 3) Deterministic decision logging: - new "policy_decision" log event emitted by the policy engine with stable, low-cardinality fields - reason codes normalized to align with 429 semantics work in gateway (QUOTA_EXCEEDED vs OVERLOAD_SHED vs ADMISSION_THROTTLE vs FALLBACK_APPLIED) 4) Validation + guardrails: - schema validation on policy load (reject unknown enums/invalid windows) - explicit checks preventing per-tenant/per-route unconstrained overrides unless allowlisted (prevents accidental fleet-wide noisy-neighbor exceptions) - budget sanity checks (burst >= rate, non-negative, max caps) 5) Tests: - golden tests for deterministic output (given the same input policy + request context, decision_hash and reason fields must match) - validation tests covering invalid policies and non-backwards-compatible changes - basic evaluation tests covering priority ordering and budget exhaustion behavior Notes on backwards compatibility: - Existing policies without priority_class/route_group continue to evaluate as STANDARD with route_group derived from route mapping if available; if not available, route_group="unknown" and budgets are not applied. - Decision logging is added but does not change existing endpoints; log event is separate from request logs. Implementation details: - Added a policy schema version bump (v7 -> v8) with a translator that fills defaults on load. - Added a small canonical route->route_group mapping table co-located with schema; enforcement services should treat route_group as authoritative once provided in request context. - Budget accounting is modeled as an input signal ("budget_remaining_bucket") for now; actual counters are managed by gateway/routing. This PR sets the language/types and ensures evaluation is deterministic. Security/Access: - Deterministic decision logging includes tenant_id but is restricted to internal logs; no customer PII. - No new external endpoints. Follow-ups (tracked separately): - Performance: caching / batch lookups for high-QPS (ENG-4924/ENG-4925 dependent, plus upcoming perf PR). - Unify reason-code taxonomy and headers with gateway implementation once landed (paired with Amara/Cole). - Add admin-only debug endpoint to inspect decisions (INT-3302) once access controls are in place. References: - Linear: ENG-4923 - Docs/Design: Hot-route capacity protection design + ADR (see Confluence links in ENG-4923) - Related PRs: gateway 429 reason codes + routing admission control will consume decision_reason + priority_class.

title: Add status summary metadata to incident events
preview:
Context
We’re implementing auto-drafted, publishable post-incident summaries (ENG-4823). The incident-bot can already export a timeline of events (see PR #19540), but the events don’t currently carry the structured metadata we need to generate consistent “Impact / Timeline / Mitigation” blocks without hand-parsing free-form notes.

This PR adds optional, structured “status summary” metadata to incident events emitted by the redwood control plane so incident-bot can draft post-incident summaries with fewer manual edits and lower risk of accidentally including sensitive detail.

What’s included
- Adds optional fields to incident event payloads:
  - impact_start (RFC3339 timestamp; when customer impact started, as determined by IC/SRE)
  - impact_end (RFC3339 timestamp; when customer impact ended / SLO recovered)
  - affected_routes (array of route identifiers, e.g. "chat.completions", "embeddings", "rerank"; intentionally coarse)
  - fallback_active (boolean; whether automatic fallback policy was active during the impact window)
- Extends the incident timeline emitter to attach these fields on relevant state transitions (Identified/Mitigating/Monitoring/Resolved) when present in the incident record.
- Adds validation/normalization:
  - impact_end must be >= impact_start
  - affected_routes is capped to a small allowlist and de-duplicated
  - timestamps are normalized to UTC
- Adds unit + golden tests for event serialization to keep incident-bot consumption stable.
- Adds a lightweight feature flag (incidents.emit_status_summary_metadata) default-off in production for staged rollout.

Non-goals
- No status page updates or incident-bot changes in this PR (those live in incident-bot / tooling PRs).
- No attempt to infer impact_start/end from metrics. These fields are explicitly set by IC/Comms Lead during incident handling.
- No inclusion of customer identifiers, tenant IDs, request IDs, or capacity details.

Implementation details
- Introduces StatusSummaryMetadata as a dedicated struct used by the incident domain model and event encoder.
- Affected routes are represented using our public API “capability” labels rather than internal service names to align with the status component taxonomy work.
- Backwards compatibility: existing consumers that ignore unknown JSON keys will continue to work.

Changed areas (high level)
- control-plane/incidents: incident model + validation
- control-plane/events: incident event encoder
- api/types: shared type definitions + docs
- tests: serialization + schema compatibility

Example event (illustrative)
{"type":"incident.state_changed","incident_id":"inc_01J...","state":"monitoring","status_summary":{"impact_start":"2025-11-24T18:12:00Z","impact_end":null,"affected_routes":["chat.completions","chat.stream"],"fallback_active":true}}

Rollout plan
1) Merge behind feature flag (this PR)
2) Enable in staging + dogfood incidents
3) Enable in prod for Sev0/Sev1 only (via config) once incident-bot parsing is deployed
4) Expand to all severities after 1–2 weeks of audits

Testing
- Unit tests for validation rules
- Golden tests for JSON payload stability
- Manual: toggled feature flag in dev environment and verified event payload contains status_summary and is omitted when disabled

Screenshots
N/A (backend-only)

Follow-ups
- incident-bot: consume these fields when drafting post-incident summary (linked to ENG-4823)
- docs: add a short internal note on how IC/Comms Lead should set impact_start/end (separate Confluence update)

Review conversation (highlights)
- Rafael Mendes: "Can we ensure affected_routes doesn’t leak internal endpoint names?" 
  - Logan Wright: "Yes — using public capability labels only; added allowlist + normalization and updated tests."
- Aisha Bello: "impact_end should be nullable until Resolved; do we want Monitoring to allow null?" 
  - Logan Wright: "Yes; Monitoring can still have null impact_end (we sometimes monitor after SLO recovers). Added explicit behavior + tests."
- Miles Thornton: "Feature flag default-off is good. Please also cap affected_routes length to avoid huge payloads." 
  - Logan Wright: "Done (max 10, de-dupe + stable ordering)."
- Christine Wong: "Can we keep fallback_active optional? Some incidents won’t have routing fallback." 
  - Logan Wright: "Kept optional; boolean only included when known, otherwise omitted."

CI
- build: pass
- unit tests: pass
- lint: pass

Outcome
Merged (squash) to main; feature flag remains off by default pending incident-bot deployment and SRE sign-off.

title: Anti-flap logic (windows + hysteresis) for status-component health signals
preview: Motivation: Our current component health rollups (used to recommend public status changes and to populate status-page dashboards) are too twitchy. Short-lived spikes (e.g., 2–3 minutes of elevated 5xx or a brief p95 regression) can flip a component between Operational ↔ Degraded multiple times, which creates noisy on-call workflows and undermines customer trust in the status page. This PR implements anti-flap windows and hysteresis in the status-signal evaluator so we only transition component state when a condition persists long enough, and we require a healthier condition to recover than the one that caused the degradation. Summary of changes: Adds a small state machine around each status signal (errors + latency) with configurable (1) trigger window (min duration / min samples) and (2) recovery window (min duration / min samples) plus (3) hysteresis thresholds (degrade_threshold vs recover_threshold). Also adds per-component overrides and emits evaluator metrics so we can audit how often anti-flap prevented a transition. Behavior details: Degrade requires threshold breach for N consecutive evaluations (default 3 of 3 at 1m cadence) OR for a minimum time window (default 5m) depending on signal type; recover requires the metric to be back under a stricter recovery threshold for a longer window (default 10m). A note on semantics: this is intentionally separate from SLO burn alerts; we’re smoothing the customer-facing component health classification, not masking genuine outages. Rollout plan: guarded behind status_signals.anti_flap_enabled (default off). In dry-run mode we compute both old/new state and publish a diff metric (status_signals_state_diff_total{component_id,signal_type}) to validate no surprises. After 1 week of dry-run we’ll enable for Hosted API components in us-east + eu-west, then expand. Docs: Updated README in the pack and added inline docs for config knobs and default values. Testing: Adds unit tests for threshold crossing, jitter, and recovery hysteresis; adds a regression test that reproduces the us-east streaming ‘toggle’ pattern from early Nov. Related: Status page transparency upgrades project; aligns with internal standard for customer-facing status signals (anti-flap guidance + thresholds).

title: Add region-aware streaming disconnect/error metrics and map to status components
preview: Context: As part of the status page transparency upgrades, we need streaming-specific signals that can be cleanly attributed to region and rolled up to public components (Hosted API > Streaming, per region). Today we have aggregate gateway error counts and some websocket-level counters, but they are not consistently labeled by region and do not distinguish client disconnects vs server-side stream aborts/timeouts, which has led to slow/incorrect status updates (see SUP-28549 and the 2025-11-07 US-East streaming degradation follow-ups). This PR adds region-aware counters for streaming failures/disconnects, standardizes label keys (region, capability=streaming, component_id), and documents how these metrics roll up into the status component health views used by on-call and incident-bot drafts. Scope: 1) Emit new metrics from the streaming path (SSE + websocket) with consistent region labeling (derived from edge ingress / routing layer). 2) Separate failure modes: client_disconnect (EOF/close), server_abort (upstream canceled), stream_timeout, and internal_error. 3) Add an aggregator helper to compute a per-region streaming error rate suitable for dashboards/alerts. 4) Add docs: what counts toward customer-facing status signals and how to interpret disconnect spikes (e.g., client disconnects should generally not drive status unless correlated with elevated server_abort/internal_error). Non-goals: changing alert thresholds or status automation behavior in this PR (that stays in observability-pack / slo-toolkit), and no changes to public status page component registry. Testing: unit tests for label derivation and error classification; integration smoke in staging with a forced upstream cancel; verified metrics presence in Prometheus and cardinality bounds in the metrics lint job. Rollout: safe to deploy behind existing runtime; metrics are additive; dashboards/alerts will be updated in follow-on PRs.
text: Context: As part of the status page transparency upgrades, we need streaming-specific signals that can be cleanly attributed to region and rolled up to public components (Hosted API > Streaming, per region). Today we have aggregate gateway error counts and some websocket-level counters, but they are not consistently labeled by region and do not distinguish client disconnects vs server-side stream aborts/timeouts, which has led to slow/incorrect status updates (see SUP-28549 and the 2025-11-07 US-East streaming degradation follow-ups). This PR adds region-aware counters for streaming failures/disconnects, standardizes label keys (region, capability=streaming, component_id), and documents how these metrics roll up into the status component health views used by on-call and incident-bot drafts. Scope: 1) Emit new metrics from the streaming path (SSE + websocket) with consistent region labeling (derived from edge ingress / routing layer). 2) Separate failure modes: client_disconnect (EOF/close), server_abort (upstream canceled), stream_timeout, and internal_error. 3) Add an aggregator helper to compute a per-region streaming error rate suitable for dashboards/alerts. 4) Add docs: what counts toward customer-facing status signals and how to interpret disconnect spikes (e.g., client disconnects should generally not drive status unless correlated with elevated server_abort/internal_error). Non-goals: changing alert thresholds or status automation behavior in this PR (that stays in observability-pack / slo-toolkit), and no changes to public status page component registry. Testing: unit tests for label derivation and error classification; integration smoke in staging with a forced upstream cancel; verified metrics presence in Prometheus and cardinality bounds in the metrics lint job. Rollout: safe to deploy behind existing runtime; metrics are additive; dashboards/alerts will be updated in follow-on PRs.

title: Emit status component tags on API gateway metrics
preview: Motivation: Status page transparency upgrades require us to attribute customer-visible symptoms (errors/latency/stream disconnects) to public components consistently. Today most of our gateway metrics are only tagged by route + model + raw service, which makes it hard to (a) drive component-level dashboards/alerts and (b) generate incident summaries that match the status page taxonomy. This PR adds low-cardinality tags to gateway metrics so the observability-pack and incident tooling can compute component health without guesswork. Scope: (1) Add tags to gateway metrics: component_id, capability, region. (2) Derive these tags from existing request context + the service-to-component mapping (no new external calls on the hot path). (3) Add guardrails to avoid cardinality explosions (no raw path tags; capability is an enum; component_id is from registry; region is from gateway config). (4) Add unit tests + a small integration test that validates tag presence on the key series used by the status classification job. Details: - component_id: computed using the existing internal routing metadata (route_kind + endpoint class) and the mapping table in gateway config; falls back to "unknown" and increments a counter when mapping is missing. - capability: one of {chat, streaming, embeddings, reranking, console, control_plane}; gateway sets streaming when request uses SSE/websocket stream mode (and uses chat otherwise). - region: injected from gateway deployment (REDWOOD_REGION) and normalized to our public labels (us-east, us-west, eu-west, ap-southeast). - Metrics updated: gateway_request_total, gateway_request_errors_total, gateway_request_duration_seconds (histogram), gateway_upstream_latency_seconds, gateway_stream_disconnects_total. Example (Prometheus): gateway_request_errors_total{component_id="hosted-api",capability="chat",region="us-east",status_code="502"} 12 Note: We explicitly do NOT add model_name or route_template as tags here; those remain available via tracing and logs. Follow-ups: After this lands, observability-pack PRs can rely on these tags for component dashboards and the weekly status accuracy audit can attribute incidents correctly. Related: ENG-4830 mapping layer + ENG-4841 component burn alerts. Testing: unit tests for tag derivation; local load test confirmed no material overhead; staging scrape verified series shape and cardinality delta is within budget.

title: Add overload shedding dashboards + initial alert rules (breaker opens, shed rate)
preview: Context: As part of the Hosted circuit breakers + load shedding reliability program, we need first-class visibility into (a) when we are shedding traffic vs failing implicitly, and (b) when breakers are opening/half-opening/closing across regions/models/tiers. This PR adds Grafana dashboards and initial Prometheus alert rules for overload protection signals. Scope: (1) Dashboards: Add "Hosted API • Overload Protection" dashboard group with panels for shed rate, breaker open rate, admission decisions, runtime saturation proxies (queue depth / token saturation / memory pressure proxy), and top shed reasons. Includes recommended drilldowns by region and route family, and a safe set of labels (bounded cardinality). (2) Alerts: Add first-pass paging + ticket alerts for (i) sustained breaker-open rate, (ii) elevated shed rate for paid tiers, (iii) Retry-After header missing on overload responses (via gateway metric), and (iv) mismatch between shed and runtime saturation (possible false positive / bad thresholds). Each alert includes runbook links and suggested next actions. (3) Runbook links: Links to oncall runbook and runtime mitigation guide; includes incident-bot channel references. Notes/guardrails: We intentionally avoid tenant_id and request_id as dimensions. Model is aggregated to a small allowlist via label mapping in the gateway metrics exporter. Dashboards default to last 1h and show p95/p99 lat overlay to correlate shedding with tail latency improvements. Follow-ups: (a) tighten alert thresholds after canary (false positives), (b) add per-tier SLO burn overlays once SLO toolkit classification is rolled out everywhere, (c) add a "top fallback attempts" panel after routing PR-18458 is fully deployed. Screenshots: See internal preview link in the Grafana folder (can be rendered in CI). Runbooks: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ONCALL/Hosted+Overload+Circuit+Breaker+Runbook and https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/SERVING/Runtime+Overload+Mitigation. Testing: `make lint`, `make test`, `make dashboards-validate`, `make prom-rules-test`.

title: Include KMS rotation audit events in export schema + parsers
preview:
Context: Redwood is adding explicit audit log events for KMS rotation and cutover toggles (see redwood PR #18541). Several regulated customers ingest Redwood audit logs into their SIEM (Splunk/Elastic/Sentinel) via this exporter. Without schema updates, these events get emitted as `unknown_event_type` and are either dropped or lose critical fields needed for rotation evidence.

This PR updates the export schema and parsing/mapping code so KMS rotation events are exported as first-class, typed records with stable fields suitable for correlation and evidence collection.

What changed (high level):
- Added new event types to the exporter schema:
  - `kms.key_version.updated`
  - `kms.rotation.cutover.enabled`
  - `kms.rotation.cutover.disabled`
  - `kms.rotation.dual_read.enabled`
  - `kms.rotation.dual_read.disabled`
- Extended the parser to recognize these events from Redwood audit log envelopes and map them into the exporter’s normalized record format.
- Added explicit field mapping for:
  - `kms_key_arn` (or provider equivalent), `key_alias` (if present)
  - `old_key_version`, `new_key_version`
  - `control_plane_scope` (hosted|dedicated|private) and `deployment_id` when present
  - `actor` / `actor_type` / `actor_email` (service vs human)
  - `change_ticket` (Linear/Jira), when populated upstream
  - `request_id` / `trace_id` passthrough for investigations
- Updated example output fixtures and docs so customers can update SIEM parsers/detection rules.
- Added unit tests and golden fixtures for each new event type, including backward compatibility behavior when optional fields are missing.

Schema/versioning:
- Bumped `schema_version` from `1.12` to `1.13`.
- Backward compatible: existing fields unchanged; new event types simply appear as additional values in `event_type` with additional optional fields.
- The exporter continues to emit `event_type=unknown` for truly unknown values, but will no longer classify the KMS rotation events as unknown.

Notable implementation details:
- Parsing logic prefers canonical fields under `payload.kms.*` but falls back to older/alternate keys when present (observed in staging): `payload.keyArn`, `payload.key_arn`, and `payload.kmsKeyArn`.
- Added strict validation for `old_key_version/new_key_version` only on `kms.key_version.updated`; cutover/dual_read toggles do not require versions.
- Added lightweight redaction guardrails: we do not export plaintext key material (none expected), and we explicitly drop any field matching `*plaintext*` or `*dek*` if upstream ever mis-sends it.

Testing:
- `go test ./...` (includes new golden fixtures)
- Added `TestParseKMSEvents_*` suite covering:
  - missing optional fields
  - unexpected provider metadata
  - unknown event passthrough remains unchanged

Rollout notes:
- No infra changes required.
- Customers consuming JSONL can deploy this exporter version and immediately see new `kms.*` events in their downstream SIEM index.
- For customers with strict schema enforcement (e.g., Sentinel DCR / Elastic data stream templates), docs include a minimal set of new fields to add.

Checklist:
- [x] Update schema and normalization map
- [x] Golden fixtures for each new event type
- [x] Docs/examples updated
- [x] Security review: confirm no key material exported

Refs:
- Redwood monorepo: PR #18541 (adds the audit events)
- Linear: ENG-4821 / ENG-4826
- Support signal: SUP-19405 (customer evidence request)
