title: Announce circuit breaker state transitions to #incidents and #eng-oncall
preview:
Context
As part of the Hosted circuit breakers + load shedding program (ENG-4821), we need a low-latency, human-visible signal when breakers start shedding (OPEN) and when they recover (HALF_OPEN/CLOSED). The current workflow relies on dashboards/alerts only; during incidents this increases time-to-diagnosis and contributes to duplicate triage.

This PR teaches incident-bot to announce breaker state transitions into Slack (#incidents and #eng-oncall) with:
- Transition summary (CLOSED → OPEN, OPEN → HALF_OPEN, HALF_OPEN → CLOSED)
- Scope (region, route, model selector, tenant/tier when available)
- Top contributing signal(s) (queue depth, token/sec saturation, dependency health)
- Links to dashboards and the oncall runbook
- A short “recommended actions” block aligned with the runbook

Behavior
- Posts OPEN transitions to both #incidents and #eng-oncall.
- Posts recovery transitions (OPEN→HALF_OPEN, HALF_OPEN→CLOSED) to #eng-oncall only by default to reduce noise.
- Dedupes repeated OPEN transitions for the same breaker_key within a configurable window (default 10m).
- If event payload does not include route/model details, message still posts with breaker_key + region and a note about missing dimensions.

Implementation details
- Adds a new event consumer for `overload.breaker_state_changed` from the internal event bus (same transport as alert notifications).
- Introduces `BreakerKey` normalization to keep dedupe stable (region + route_group + model_selector + tier + policy_id).
- Adds Slack message formatting using Block Kit, including:
  - severity coloring (OPEN = danger, HALF_OPEN = warning, CLOSED = good)
  - buttons: “Open dashboard”, “Open runbook”, “Search logs”
- Uses `dashboards.redwood.ai` and `runbooks.redwood.ai` deep links with query params prefilled (region, time range last 30m, breaker_key).

Recommended actions (message content)
- OPEN: Verify whether this is capacity saturation vs downstream brownout; check shed-rate + runtime queue depth; confirm Retry-After is being set; page routing owner if fallback loops suspected.
- HALF_OPEN: Watch error budget burn + shed rate; do not raise caps until p95 stabilizes.
- CLOSED: Capture incident timestamp window; confirm alerts cleared; annotate postmortem with breaker_key and top signals.

Safety/guardrails
- Hard cap of 1 Slack message per breaker_key per channel per 10 minutes (configurable).
- Suppresses messages for canary-only breakers unless `announce_canary=true` is set (default false).
- Sanitizes tenant IDs: only posts tenant slug if explicitly marked as `shareable=true` in the event payload.

Testing
- Unit tests for normalization, dedupe window, channel routing, and link generation.
- Integration-style test with a mocked Slack client to validate Block Kit payload.

Rollout
- Deploy behind flag `INCIDENT_BOT_BREAKER_ANNOUNCEMENTS=enabled`.
- Start enabled in staging; prod enablement to follow canary rollout go/no-go email thread.

Screenshots (example Slack message)
[OPEN] Hosted breaker opened: us-east / chat.completions / model=rw-llama-3-70b (tier=self-serve)
Signals: runtime.queue_depth_p95=182, token_saturation=0.93
Shed rate: 14% (last 5m)
Actions: Check Overload Protection dashboard; consider temporary max_tokens cap policy; verify Retry-After present; evaluate fallback policy health
Buttons: Open dashboard | Open runbook | Search logs

Notes
- This PR does not change alerting rules; it is a complementary “human paging context” path.
- Follow-up: thread-safe in-memory dedupe should move to Redis if we run multiple incident-bot replicas (tracked in ENG-4869).

title: runtime: export scheduler queue depth + saturation metrics (bounded cardinality)
preview:
Context: As part of hosted overload protection work, SRE + routing need a small set of reliable, low-cardinality runtime saturation signals that correlate with tail latency / admission failures. Today we have a few high-cardinality per-worker metrics (and gaps for queue depth) that are difficult to alert on and have caused Prom churn during bursts.

This PR adds scheduler queue depth + saturation gauges to the serving runtime and introduces an aggregation layer to bound metric cardinality. These are intended to be the primary signals consumed by the gateway/policy engine and dashboards (shed rate vs saturation), and to support dark-launch tuning.

What’s in this PR:
- New runtime metrics emitted from the scheduler:
  - runtime_sched_prefill_queue_depth (gauge)
  - runtime_sched_decode_queue_depth (gauge)
  - runtime_sched_inflight_requests (gauge)
  - runtime_sched_token_throughput_utilization (gauge, 0..1) — computed as EWMA(decode_tokens_per_sec) / configured_decode_capacity
  - runtime_sched_kv_cache_pressure (gauge, 0..1) — derived from kv allocator watermarks + eviction rate
  - runtime_sched_gpu_mem_pressure (gauge, 0..1) — uses fragmentation proxy + allocator fail retries (see notes below)
- Aggregation / cardinality bounding:
  - Metrics are exported at a “pool summary” level (gpu_pool_id, model_family, region, runtime_build) rather than per pod/worker.
  - model is normalized to model_family (e.g., "llama3-8b", "mistral-7b") to avoid per-version/cardinality spikes.
  - per-tenant labels are explicitly forbidden in the runtime emitter (compile-time allowlist).
  - A small in-process aggregator periodically flushes summary metrics; per-worker series remain available only behind an internal debug flag.
- Safety + rollout:
  - Behind flag: runtime.metrics.saturation_v2 (default off). When off, runtime emits existing metrics only.
  - When on, both old + new metrics emit for one release cycle to allow dashboard migration.
- Docs/tests:
  - Added unit tests for queue accounting and EWMA utilization math.
  - Added a "cardinality budget" test that fails if labelset exceeds the allowlist.

Notes on the signals:
- Queue depth is split into prefill vs decode because we’ve seen different failure modes (prefill spikes correlate with long prompts; decode saturation correlates with sustained concurrency and streaming).
- gpu_mem_pressure is not a raw NVML % (too noisy across mixed workloads) — it’s a normalized proxy combining allocator watermark + retry count. We can revise after we compare to incident data.

Example (Prom exposition):
- runtime_sched_decode_queue_depth{region="us-east",gpu_pool_id="hosted-a10",model_family="llama3-8b",runtime_build="2025.11.0"} 42
- runtime_sched_token_throughput_utilization{region="us-east",gpu_pool_id="hosted-a10",model_family="llama3-8b",runtime_build="2025.11.0"} 0.93

Follow-ups (not in this PR):
- Update dashboards/alerts to use new signals (tracked in ENG-4826; observability-pack PR is separate).
- Wire these into routing/policy evaluation (tracked in ENG-4823/ENG-4824).

Risk:
- Low-to-moderate. Metric emission is gated; aggregator runs on a 5s tick and is designed to be O(1) per update with bounded memory. We validated no additional locks on the hot decode path beyond atomic counters.

Validation:
- Ran local replay with bursty prompt distribution; queue depth and utilization track expected load ramps.
- Verified metric series count stays flat (< 400 series per region/pool/model_family) under 20 model versions.

References:
- Linear: ENG-4825
- Program: Hosted circuit breakers + load shedding (Reliability Program)

  - When on, both old + new metrics emit for one release cycle to allow dashboard migration.
- Docs/tests:
  - Added unit tests for queue accounting and EWMA utilization math.
  - Added a "cardinality budget" test that fails if labelset exceeds the allowlist.

Notes on the signals:
- Queue depth is split into prefill vs decode because we’ve seen different failure modes (prefill spikes correlate with long prompts; decode saturation correlates with sustained concurrency and streaming).
- gpu_mem_pressure is not a raw NVML % (too noisy across mixed workloads) — it’s a normalized proxy combining allocator watermark + retry count. We can revise after we compare to incident data.

Example (Prom exposition):
- runtime_sched_decode_queue_depth{region="us-east",gpu_pool_id="hosted-a10",model_family="llama3-8b",runtime_build="2025.11.0"} 42
- runtime_sched_token_throughput_utilization{region="us-east",gpu_pool_id="hosted-a10",model_family="llama3-8b",runtime_build="2025.11.0"} 0.93

Follow-ups (not in this PR):
- Update dashboards/alerts to use new signals (tracked in ENG-4826; observability-pack PR is separate).
- Wire these into routing/policy evaluation (tracked in ENG-4823/ENG-4824).

Risk:
- Low-to-moderate. Metric emission is gated; aggregator runs on a 5s tick and is designed to be O(1) per update with bounded memory. We validated no additional locks on the hot decode path beyond atomic counters.

Validation:
- Ran local replay with bursty prompt distribution; queue depth and utilization track expected load ramps.
- Verified metric series count stays flat (< 400 series per region/pool/model_family) under 20 model versions.

References:
- Linear: ENG-4825
- Program: Hosted circuit breakers + load shedding (Reliability Program)

title: Add KMS latency/error dashboards + alert rules (per env/region)
preview: Context: As part of the KMS integration hardening initiative we needed first-class dependency visibility for KMS across Hosted/Dedicated/Private paths. This PR adds a KMS Dependency Health dashboard and alert rules that align to the canonical error taxonomy (AccessDenied/NotFound/Throttling/Timeout/InvalidCiphertext/Unknown) and our metric namespacing conventions. Scope: (1) Dashboard: Overview + per env/region panels for request volume, latency (p50/p95/p99), error rate by category, throttling signals, and circuit breaker state; (2) Alerts: page-worthy vs ticket-worthy thresholds, with environment-aware routing (prod pages, staging tickets) and burn-rate style windows where appropriate; (3) Operator ergonomics: runbook links + log/trace pivot hints (trace_id, kms_key_id, provider, region, service); (4) Documentation: short README explaining how to use/tune the alerts and what to check first during an incident. Recommended thresholds (initial): Latency p95: warn >250ms for 10m, page >500ms for 5m (prod only); Timeout error rate: warn >0.25% for 10m, page >0.75% for 5m; Throttling: warn >0.5% for 10m, page >1.5% for 10m (with annotation to check retry storm/backpressure); AccessDenied: page on sustained >10/min for 5m (prod) because it often indicates mis-scoped IAM or a rollout regression; InvalidCiphertext: page on any sustained nonzero (>1/min for 5m) because it can indicate key mismatch/rotation cutover issues. Notes: thresholds are intentionally conservative and will be tuned post-rollout (see ENG-4828 follow-up and the alert noise discussion). This PR assumes the KMS metrics emitted by the runtime/control plane are available (redwood PR-18434 / PR-18515 / PR-18527) and uses the standardized tags: env, region, service, provider, error_category, error_code, kms_key_id (hashed).

title: Dashboards: correlate model version change rate with rollout phases and incident/regression signals
preview: Context: As part of the model metadata schema + changelog generator rollout, we now emit structured model change events (promotions, rollbacks, metadata edits) and rollout phase transitions. Support and oncall repeatedly asked for a fast way to answer: “did this latency/regression coincide with a model version promotion or rollout phase change?” This PR adds Grafana panels + recording rules to correlate: (1) model version change rate, (2) rollout phase transitions (canary/A-B/GA/rollback), and (3) incident/regression signals (5xx, timeouts, p95/p99 latency, eval-guardrail failures when present). What’s included: - New dashboard: “Model Rollout Correlation” with panels for change rate over time, rollout phase annotations, and ‘before/after’ latency + error overlays. - Update existing “LLM Serving Overview” dashboard with a compact panel showing model version change rate by deployment_mode and model_name (top 10) plus a click-through to the correlation dashboard. - Prometheus recording rules to normalize and de-cardinalize model change counters for dashboards (avoid high-cardinality labels from raw events). - Standard annotations schema for rollout phase changes (Grafana annotations sourced from Loki logs) to visualize canary/A-B boundaries. Notes/assumptions: - This depends on control-plane emitting model_change and rollout_phase_change events (redwood PR #18435 / #18458). In environments where those logs/metrics aren’t present yet, panels will render ‘No data’ but won’t break dashboards. - We intentionally do not include per-customer/account identifiers in any metric labelset; correlation for account-specific cases should use trace IDs / audit tooling, not dashboards. - Private deployments: these dashboards assume the “control-plane-events” log stream is configured; otherwise skip the annotations panel per docs added here.

title: Audit log: emit model change events + internal admin query endpoints
preview:
Problem: Support/Security need authoritative, queryable audit history for model metadata changes (create/update/promote/deprecate/rollback) to answer enterprise escalations (e.g., “what changed, who did it, when, and in which deployment?”) and to satisfy audit/retention expectations for Private/VPC. Today we emit some control-plane events for changelog generation, but they’re not shaped as audit events, not consistently retained, and there’s no internal support surface to query by model/version/workspace.

This PR adds (1) first-class audit log events for model metadata and promotion lifecycle, and (2) an internal admin-only query API used by Support/Compliance to retrieve the audit timeline. Customer-visible release notes remain generated elsewhere; this is the compliance/support trace.

Scope:
- Emit audit events on: model_version.created, model_version.metadata_updated, model_version.promoted, model_version.deprecated, model_version.rollback_initiated, model_version.rollback_completed.
- Ensure events include actor + request context (service/user), are redact-safe (no internal-only fields leak), and are linkable to model IDs/version IDs.
- Add internal admin endpoints to query audit history with pagination and filters; gated behind RBAC permission `auditlog:read:model_changes` and additionally `admin_only=true` (no customer roles).
- Wire audit events into the existing AuditLog pipeline (retention, SIEM export) with a dedicated event namespace `model_registry.*`.

Non-goals:
- Customer subscriptions/notifications.
- Rewriting historical events (we’ll have partial backfill in a follow-up; this PR is forward-looking).

Event contract (high level):
- `event_type`: one of the above
- `event_id`: UUID (idempotency keyed by (request_id, event_type, object_id))
- `timestamp`
- `actor`: {type=user|service, id, email (if user), service_name (if service)}
- `workspace_id` (nullable for internal catalog ops)
- `object`: {kind=model_version, model_id, model_slug, version_id, version_semver}
- `deployment_context`: {hosted|dedicated|private, region (optional), private_cluster_id (optional)}
- `change_summary`: human-readable, redaction-safe summary
- `diff`: stored as internal-only JSON in the audit store; API returns a redacted view unless caller has `auditlog:read:model_changes_internal`

Admin API:
- GET /internal/admin/audit/model-changes?model_id=&version_id=&workspace_id=&event_type=&after=&limit=
- GET /internal/admin/audit/model-changes/{event_id}
Both endpoints are available only to `RedwoodEmployee` principals and service accounts in the `support-tooling` group.

Notes on redaction:
- We never include `internal_notes`, `embargo_reason`, or raw metadata blobs in the default response.
- For internal security/compliance roles, we return a structured `diff_preview` containing only approved fields (field allowlist).

Follow-ups:
- Add UI in Support Admin (separate PR) + docs/runbook for incident responders.
- Backfill best-effort events for the last 30 days using changelog generator store.

Refs:
- Linear: ENG-9828 (audit log events for model metadata changes), ENG-9825 (redaction rules)
- Jira: INT-11903 (support admin access request), SUP-28429 (customer reports output regression after silent model change)
- Related PRs: pr-18435 (control-plane change events), pr-18447 (console APIs), pr-18452 (CI preview), pr-18441 (generator templates)

Checklist:
- [x] Security review requested (Marcus)
- [x] RBAC policy updated + tests
- [x] Event payloads validated against audit schema
- [x] Load test: 1k events/min sustained for 10 min w/ no dropped events
- [x] Runbook snippet added to internal docs (link in comments)
- [x] Metrics + alerts added for event ingestion lag
